### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Bangladesh](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Bangladesh "Foreign relations of Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Diplomatic_relations_of_Bangladesh.svg/250px-Diplomatic_relations_of_Bangladesh.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Diplomatic_relations_of_Bangladesh.svg)

Countries that share diplomatic relations with Bangladesh

Bangladesh is considered a [middle power](/wiki/Middle_power "Middle power") in [global politics](/wiki/Global_politics "Global politics").[[138]](#cite_note-140) It plays an important role in the geopolitical affairs of the [Indo-Pacific](/wiki/Indo-Pacific "Indo-Pacific"),[[139]](#cite_note-141) due to its strategic location between South and Southeast Asia.[[140]](#cite_note-142) Bangladesh joined the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") in 1972 and the United Nations in 1974.[[141]](#cite_note-143)[[142]](#cite_note-144) It relies on multilateral diplomacy on issues like climate change, nuclear non-proliferation, trade policy and non-traditional security issues.[[143]](#cite_note-145) Bangladesh pioneered the creation of [SAARC](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation"), the preeminent forum for regional diplomacy on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").[[144]](#cite_note-146) It joined the [OIC](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") in 1974,[[145]](#cite_note-147) and is a founding member of the [Developing-8](/wiki/D-8_Organization_for_Economic_Cooperation "D-8 Organization for Economic Cooperation").[[146]](#cite_note-148) Recently, Bangladesh has focused on promoting regional trade and transport links with support from the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank").[[147]](#cite_note-149) It is also [attempting](/wiki/Accession_of_Bangladesh_to_ASEAN "Accession of Bangladesh to ASEAN") to join [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") as one of its top foreign policy goals.[[148]](#cite_note-:5-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151)[[150]](#cite_note-152) Dhaka hosts the headquarters of [BIMSTEC](/wiki/BIMSTEC "BIMSTEC"), an organisation that brings together [countries dependent on the Bay of Bengal](/wiki/Countries_dependent_on_the_Bay_of_Bengal "Countries dependent on the Bay of Bengal").

[Relations](/wiki/Bangladesh%E2%80%93Myanmar_relations "Bangladesh–Myanmar relations") with neighbouring Myanmar have been severely strained since 2016–2017, after over 700,000 [Rohingya refugees](/wiki/Rohingya_refugees_in_Bangladesh "Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh") illegally entered Bangladesh.[[151]](#cite_note-RohingyaHRW-153) Bangladesh's parliament, government, and civil society have been at the forefront of [international criticism](/wiki/International_reaction_to_the_2016%E2%80%9317_Rohingya_exodus "International reaction to the 2016–17 Rohingya exodus") against Myanmar for military operations against the Rohingya, and have demanded their [right of return](/wiki/Right_of_return "Right of return") to [Arakan](/wiki/Arakan "Arakan").[[152]](#cite_note-154)[[153]](#cite_note-155)

Bangladesh shares an important [bilateral and economic relationship](/wiki/Bangladesh%E2%80%93India_relations "Bangladesh–India relations") with its largest neighbour, India,[[154]](#cite_note-156) which is often strained by [water politics](/wiki/Water_politics_in_India "Water politics in India") of the [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges_water_dispute "Ganges water dispute") and the [Teesta](/wiki/Teesta_River#Water_sharing_challenge "Teesta River"),[[155]](#cite_note-157)[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159) and the [border killings of Bangladeshi civilians](/wiki/Deaths_along_the_Bangladesh%E2%80%93India_border "Deaths along the Bangladesh–India border").[[158]](#cite_note-160)[[159]](#cite_note-161) Bangladesh's relationship with Pakistan is problematic, mainly because Pakistan denies the 1971 [Bangladesh genocide](/wiki/Bangladesh_genocide "Bangladesh genocide").[[160]](#cite_note-162) It maintains a [warm relationship](/wiki/Bangladesh-China_relations "Bangladesh-China relations") with China, its largest trading partner and largest arms supplier.[[161]](#cite_note-163) Japan is Bangladesh's largest economic aid provider, and the two have a [strategic and economic partnership](/wiki/Bangladesh%E2%80%93Japan_relations "Bangladesh–Japan relations").[[162]](#cite_note-164) Political relations with Middle Eastern countries are robust.[[163]](#cite_note-165) Bangladesh receives 59% of its remittances from the Middle East,[[164]](#cite_note-166) despite poor working conditions affecting over four million [Bangladeshi workers](/wiki/Bangladeshis_in_the_Middle_East "Bangladeshis in the Middle East").[[165]](#cite_note-167) Bangladesh plays a major role in [global climate](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") diplomacy as a leader of the [Climate Vulnerable Forum](/wiki/Climate_Vulnerable_Forum "Climate Vulnerable Forum").[[166]](#cite_note-168)

### Military

Main article: [Bangladesh Armed Forces](/wiki/Bangladesh_Armed_Forces "Bangladesh Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Bangladeshi_soldiers_on_a_BTR-80_APC.jpg/250px-Bangladeshi_soldiers_on_a_BTR-80_APC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangladeshi_soldiers_on_a_BTR-80_APC.jpg)

Bangladesh Army personnel on a [BTR-80](/wiki/BTR-80 "BTR-80")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/BNS_Bongobondhu2.jpg/250px-BNS_Bongobondhu2.jpg)](/wiki/File:BNS_Bongobondhu2.jpg)

Bangladesh Navy [Frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate"), [BNS Khalid Bin Walid](/wiki/BNS_Khalid_Bin_Walid "BNS Khalid Bin Walid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Mikoyan_MiG-29-Chengdu_F-7.jpg/250px-Mikoyan_MiG-29-Chengdu_F-7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mikoyan_MiG-29-Chengdu_F-7.jpg)

Mig-29 and F-7, Fighter aircraft's of Bangladesh Air Force

The [Bangladesh Armed Forces](/wiki/Bangladesh_Armed_Forces "Bangladesh Armed Forces") have inherited the institutional framework of the [British military](/wiki/British_military "British military") and the [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army").[[167]](#cite_note-169) In 2024, the active personnel strength of the [Bangladesh Armed Forces](/wiki/Bangladesh_Armed_Forces "Bangladesh Armed Forces") was around 230,000,[[168]](#cite_note-IISS-170) including the Air Force (21,000) and the Navy (27,000).[[169]](#cite_note-171) In addition to traditional defence roles, the military has supported civil authorities in disaster relief and provided internal security during periods of political unrest. For many years, Bangladesh has been the world's largest contributor to [UN peacekeeping forces](/wiki/United_Nations_peacekeeping "United Nations peacekeeping").[[170]](#cite_note-172) The [military budget of Bangladesh](/wiki/Military_budget_of_Bangladesh "Military budget of Bangladesh") accounts for 1.3% of GDP, amounting to US$4.3 billion in 2021.[[171]](#cite_note-173)[[172]](#cite_note-174)

The [Bangladesh Navy](/wiki/Bangladesh_Navy "Bangladesh Navy"), one of the largest in the Bay of Bengal, includes a [fleet](/wiki/List_of_active_ships_of_the_Bangladesh_Navy "List of active ships of the Bangladesh Navy") of frigates, submarines, corvettes, and other vessels. The [Bangladesh Air Force](/wiki/Bangladesh_Air_Force "Bangladesh Air Force") has a [small fleet](/wiki/List_of_active_Bangladesh_military_aircraft "List of active Bangladesh military aircraft") of multi-role combat aircraft. Most of Bangladesh's military equipment comes from China.[[173]](#cite_note-175) In recent years, Bangladesh and India have increased joint military exercises, high-level visits of military leaders, [counter-terrorism](/wiki/Counter-terrorism "Counter-terrorism") cooperation and intelligence sharing. Bangladesh is vital to ensuring stability and security in [northeast India](/wiki/Northeast_India "Northeast India").[[174]](#cite_note-176)[[175]](#cite_note-177)

Bangladesh's strategic importance in the eastern subcontinent hinges on its proximity to China, its frontier with Burma, the separation of mainland and northeast India, and its maritime territory in the Bay of Bengal.[[176]](#cite_note-178) In 2002, Bangladesh and China signed a Defence Cooperation Agreement.[[177]](#cite_note-179) The United States has pursued negotiations with Bangladesh on a [Status of forces agreement](/wiki/Status_of_forces_agreement "Status of forces agreement"), an [Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement](/wiki/Acquisition_and_Cross-Servicing_Agreement "Acquisition and Cross-Servicing Agreement") and a General Security of Military Information Agreement.[[178]](#cite_note-180)[[179]](#cite_note-181)[[180]](#cite_note-182) In 2019, Bangladesh ratified the UN [Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[181]](#cite_note-183)

### Civil society

Main article: [List of non-governmental organisations in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_non-governmental_organisations_in_Bangladesh "List of non-governmental organisations in Bangladesh")

Since the colonial period, Bangladesh has had a prominent [civil society](/wiki/Civil_society "Civil society"). There are various special interest groups, including [non-governmental organisations](/wiki/Non-governmental_organisations "Non-governmental organisations"), human rights organisations, professional associations, [chambers of commerce](/wiki/Chamber_of_commerce "Chamber of commerce"), employers' associations, and trade unions.[[182]](#cite_note-184) The [National Human Rights Commission of Bangladesh](/wiki/National_Human_Rights_Commission_of_Bangladesh "National Human Rights Commission of Bangladesh") was set up in 2007. Notable human rights organisations and initiatives include the [Centre for Law and Mediation](/wiki/Centre_for_Law_and_Mediation_(Bangladesh) "Centre for Law and Mediation (Bangladesh)"), [Odhikar](/wiki/Odhikar "Odhikar"), the [Alliance for Bangladesh Worker Safety](/wiki/Alliance_for_Bangladesh_Worker_Safety "Alliance for Bangladesh Worker Safety"), the [Bangladesh Environmental Lawyers Association](/wiki/Bangladesh_Environmental_Lawyers_Association "Bangladesh Environmental Lawyers Association"), the [Bangladesh Hindu Buddhist Christian Unity Council](/wiki/Bangladesh_Hindu_Buddhist_Christian_Unity_Council "Bangladesh Hindu Buddhist Christian Unity Council") and the [War Crimes Fact Finding Committee](/wiki/War_Crimes_Fact_Finding_Committee "War Crimes Fact Finding Committee"). The world's largest international NGO [BRAC](/wiki/BRAC_(organisation) "BRAC (organisation)") is based in Bangladesh. There has been concern about the shrinking space for independent civil society in recent years.[[183]](#cite_note-185)[[184]](#cite_note-186)[[185]](#cite_note-187)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Bangladesh](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Bangladesh "Human rights in Bangladesh")

[![Armed men in black uniforms on a street](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/R.A.B.jpg/250px-R.A.B.jpg)](/wiki/File:R.A.B.jpg)

The [Rapid Action Battalion](/wiki/Rapid_Action_Battalion "Rapid Action Battalion") (RAB) have been widely accused of [extrajudicial killings](/wiki/Extrajudicial_killing "Extrajudicial killing"), [enforced disappearances](/wiki/Enforced_disappearance "Enforced disappearance") and human right abuses. The [United States Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") sanctioned RAB in 2021.[[186]](#cite_note-188)

[Torture](/wiki/Torture "Torture") is banned by the [Constitution of Bangladesh](/wiki/Constitution_of_Bangladesh "Constitution of Bangladesh"),[[187]](#cite_note-189) but is rampantly used by Bangladesh's security forces. Bangladesh joined the [Convention against Torture](/wiki/Convention_against_Torture "Convention against Torture") in 1998 and enacted its first anti-torture law, the [Torture and Custodial Death (Prevention) Act](/wiki/Torture_and_Custodial_Death_(Prevention)_Act "Torture and Custodial Death (Prevention) Act"), in 2013. The first conviction under this law was announced in 2020.[[188]](#cite_note-190) Amnesty International [Prisoners of Conscience](/wiki/Prisoner_of_conscience "Prisoner of conscience") from Bangladesh have included [Saber Hossain Chowdhury](/wiki/Saber_Hossain_Chowdhury "Saber Hossain Chowdhury") and [Shahidul Alam](/wiki/Shahidul_Alam "Shahidul Alam").[[189]](#cite_note-191)[[190]](#cite_note-192) The widely criticised [Digital Security Act](/wiki/Digital_Security_Act "Digital Security Act") was repealed and replaced by the Cyber Security Act in 2023.[[191]](#cite_note-193) The repeal was welcomed by the [International Press Institute](/wiki/International_Press_Institute "International Press Institute").[[192]](#cite_note-194)

On [International Human Rights Day](/wiki/International_Human_Rights_Day "International Human Rights Day") in December 2021, the [United States Department of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") announced [sanctions](/wiki/Economic_sanctions "Economic sanctions") on commanders of the [Rapid Action Battalion](/wiki/Rapid_Action_Battalion "Rapid Action Battalion") for extrajudicial killings, torture, and other human rights abuses.[[193]](#cite_note-195) [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") has criticised the government for human rights abuses, the crackdown on the opposition, mass media, and civil society through politicised enforcement.[[194]](#cite_note-196) Bangladesh is ranked "partly free" in Freedom House's *[Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World")* report,[[195]](#cite_note-197) but its [press freedom](/wiki/Press_freedom "Press freedom") has deteriorated from "free" to "not free" in recent years due to increasing government pressure.[[196]](#cite_note-198) According to the British [Economist Intelligence Unit](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit"), the country has a [hybrid regime](/wiki/Hybrid_regime "Hybrid regime"): the third of four rankings in its [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index").[[197]](#cite_note-199) Bangladesh ranked 96th among 163 countries in the 2022 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[198]](#cite_note-200) According to National Human Rights Commission, 70% of alleged human-rights violations are committed by law-enforcement agencies.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

[LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Bangladesh "LGBT rights in Bangladesh") are frowned upon among social conservatives.[[200]](#cite_note-202) Homosexuality is affected by [Section 377](/wiki/Section_377 "Section 377") of the [Penal Code of Bangladesh](/wiki/Penal_Code_of_Bangladesh "Penal Code of Bangladesh"), originally enacted by the British colonial government.[[201]](#cite_note-203)[[202]](#cite_note-204) The government only recognises the [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") and [intersex](/wiki/Intersex "Intersex") community known as the [Hijra](/wiki/Hijra_(South_Asia) "Hijra (South Asia)").[[203]](#cite_note-205)[[204]](#cite_note-206) According to the 2023 Global Slavery Index, an estimated 1.2 million people were enslaved in Bangladesh as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit), which is among the highest in the world.[[205]](#cite_note-207)

### Corruption

Main article: [Corruption in Bangladesh](/wiki/Corruption_in_Bangladesh "Corruption in Bangladesh")

Like many developing countries, [institutional corruption](/wiki/Institutional_corruption "Institutional corruption") is an issue of concern for Bangladesh. Bangladesh ranked 146th among 180 countries on [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International")'s 2018 [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index").[[206]](#cite_note-208) Land administration was the sector with the most bribery in 2015,[[207]](#cite_note-209) followed by education,[[208]](#cite_note-210) police[[209]](#cite_note-211) and water supply.[[210]](#cite_note-212) The [Anti Corruption Commission](/wiki/Anti_Corruption_Commission_Bangladesh "Anti Corruption Commission Bangladesh") was formed in 2004, and was active during the [2006–08 Bangladeshi political crisis](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9308_Bangladeshi_political_crisis "2006–08 Bangladeshi political crisis"), indicting many leading politicians, bureaucrats and businessmen for [graft](/wiki/Graft_(politics) "Graft (politics)").[[211]](#cite_note-213)[[212]](#cite_note-214)[[213]](#cite_note-215)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Bangladesh](/wiki/Economy_of_Bangladesh "Economy of Bangladesh")

Further information: [List of companies of Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Bangladesh "List of companies of Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Dhaka_April_%2833244268934%29.jpg/250px-Dhaka_April_%2833244268934%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dhaka_April_(33244268934).jpg)

[Motijheel](/wiki/Motijheel_Thana "Motijheel Thana") in the nation's capital Dhaka is the largest [commercial district](/wiki/Central_business_district "Central business district") in the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/The_padma_bridge_04.jpg/250px-The_padma_bridge_04.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_padma_bridge_04.jpg)

The [Padma Bridge](/wiki/Padma_Bridge "Padma Bridge"), opened in 2022, is a [road-rail bridge](/wiki/List_of_road%E2%80%93rail_bridges "List of road–rail bridges") which spans the [Padma River](/wiki/Padma_River "Padma River").

Bangladesh is a [lower-middle](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GNI_(nominal)_per_capita#Lower-middle-income_group "List of countries by GNI (nominal) per capita") income [mixed-market economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy").[[214]](#cite_note-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217) It has the world's [36th-largest economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") by nominal terms, and the [24th-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") by [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity"). Bangladesh has a [labour force](/wiki/Labour_force "Labour force") of 71.4 million,[[216]](#cite_note-218) which is the world's [seventh-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_labour_force "List of countries by labour force"); with an unemployment rate of 3.6% as of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit).[[217]](#cite_note-219) Its [foreign exchange reserves](/wiki/Foreign_exchange_reserves "Foreign exchange reserves"), although depleting,[[218]](#cite_note-220) remain the [second-highest in South Asia](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_foreign_exchange_reserves "List of countries by foreign exchange reserves"), after India. Bangladesh's [large diaspora](/wiki/Bangladeshi_diaspora "Bangladeshi diaspora") contributed roughly $27 billion in [remittances](/wiki/Remittance_to_Bangladesh "Remittance to Bangladesh") in 2024.[[219]](#cite_note-221) The [Bangladeshi taka](/wiki/Bangladeshi_taka "Bangladeshi taka") is the national currency.

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit), the large service sector accounts for about 51.5% of total GDP, followed by the industrial sector (34.6%), while the [agriculture sector](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Bangladesh "Agriculture in Bangladesh") is by far the smallest, making up only 11% of total GDP;[[8]](#cite_note-cia-8) despite being the largest employment sector, providing roughly half of the total workforce.[[220]](#cite_note-222) Over 84% of the export earnings come from the [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry_in_Bangladesh "Textile industry in Bangladesh").[[221]](#cite_note-223) Bangladesh is the second-leading garments exporter in the world, and plays a crucial role in the global [fast fashion](/wiki/Fast_fashion "Fast fashion") industry, exporting to various Western fashion brands.[[222]](#cite_note-224) It is also a major producer of [jute](/wiki/Jute_industry_of_Bangladesh "Jute industry of Bangladesh"),[[223]](#cite_note-225) [rice](/wiki/Rice_production_in_Bangladesh "Rice production in Bangladesh"),[[224]](#cite_note-226) [fish](/wiki/List_of_fishes_in_Bangladesh "List of fishes in Bangladesh"),[[225]](#cite_note-227) [tea](/wiki/Tea_production_in_Bangladesh "Tea production in Bangladesh"),[[226]](#cite_note-228) and flowers.[[227]](#cite_note-229) Other major industries include [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding_in_Bangladesh "Shipbuilding in Bangladesh"), [pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry_in_Bangladesh "Pharmaceutical industry in Bangladesh"), [steel](/wiki/Steel_industry_in_Bangladesh "Steel industry in Bangladesh"), [electronics](/wiki/Electronics_industry_in_Bangladesh "Electronics industry in Bangladesh") and [leather goods](/wiki/Leather_industry_in_Bangladesh "Leather industry in Bangladesh").[[228]](#cite_note-230) China is the [largest trading partner](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_trading_partners_of_Bangladesh "List of the largest trading partners of Bangladesh") of Bangladesh, accounting for 15% of the total trade, followed by India; which accounts for 8% of the total trade.[[229]](#cite_note-231)

The private sector accounts for 80% of GDP compared to the dwindling role of state-owned companies.[[230]](#cite_note-232) Bangladesh's economy is dominated by family-owned [conglomerates](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Bangladesh "List of companies of Bangladesh") and small and medium-sized businesses. Some of the largest publicly traded companies in Bangladesh include [BEXIMCO](/wiki/BEXIMCO "BEXIMCO"), [BRAC Bank](/wiki/BRAC_Bank "BRAC Bank"), [BSRM](/wiki/BSRM "BSRM"), [GPH Ispat](/wiki/GPH_Ispat "GPH Ispat"), [Grameenphone](/wiki/Grameenphone "Grameenphone"), [Summit Group](/wiki/Summit_Group "Summit Group"), and [Square Pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Square_Pharmaceuticals "Square Pharmaceuticals").[[231]](#cite_note-233) The [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka_Stock_Exchange "Dhaka Stock Exchange") and [Chittagong Stock Exchanges](/wiki/Chittagong_Stock_Exchange "Chittagong Stock Exchange") are the country's twin [capital markets](/wiki/Capital_market "Capital market").[[232]](#cite_note-234) Its [telecommunications industry](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Bangladesh "Telecommunications in Bangladesh") is one of the world's fastest growing, with 188.78 million cellphone subscribers at the end of November 2024.[[233]](#cite_note-235) Political instability,[[234]](#cite_note-236) high inflation,[[235]](#cite_note-sadiq-237) [endemic corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Bangladesh "Corruption in Bangladesh"),[[236]](#cite_note-238) insufficient power supplies,[[237]](#cite_note-239) and slow implementation of reforms are major challenges to economic growth.[[235]](#cite_note-sadiq-237)

Bangladesh was ranked 106th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2024 and in 2025.[[238]](#cite_note-240)

### Energy

Main article: [Electricity sector in Bangladesh](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_Bangladesh "Electricity sector in Bangladesh")

Further information: [Energy policy of Bangladesh](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_Bangladesh "Energy policy of Bangladesh"), [Bangladesh Power Development Board](/wiki/Bangladesh_Power_Development_Board "Bangladesh Power Development Board"), [Natural gas and petroleum in Bangladesh](/wiki/Natural_gas_and_petroleum_in_Bangladesh "Natural gas and petroleum in Bangladesh"), and [Petrobangla](/wiki/Petrobangla "Petrobangla")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Ruppur_Nuclear_Power_Plant_%2C_Ruppur%2C_Pabna.jpg/250px-Ruppur_Nuclear_Power_Plant_%2C_Ruppur%2C_Pabna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruppur_Nuclear_Power_Plant_,_Ruppur,_Pabna.jpg)

The under-construction [Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Rooppur_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant") at night

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Moving_Turbines%21%21.jpg/250px-Moving_Turbines%21%21.jpg)](/wiki/File:Moving_Turbines!!.jpg)

Wind turbines on [Kutubdia Island](/wiki/Kutubdia_Island "Kutubdia Island")

Bangladesh, a country experiencing daily blackouts several times a day in 2009, achieved 100% electrification by 2022.[[239]](#cite_note-241)[[240]](#cite_note-242) It is gradually transitioning to a [green economy](/wiki/Green_economy "Green economy") and has the largest off-grid solar power programme in the world, benefiting 20 million people.[[241]](#cite_note-243) An [electric car](/wiki/Electric_car "Electric car") called the *Palki* is being developed for production in the country.[[242]](#cite_note-244) [Biogas](/wiki/Biogas "Biogas") is being used to produce organic fertiliser.[[243]](#cite_note-245) The under-construction [Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant](/wiki/Rooppur_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant"), under-construction with assistance from the Russian company [Rosatom](/wiki/Rosatom "Rosatom"), will be the first operational [nuclear power plant](/wiki/Nuclear_power_plant "Nuclear power plant") in the country. Its first unit, out of the two total units, is expected to go into operation in 2025.[[244]](#cite_note-246)

Bangladesh continues to have huge untapped reserves of natural gas, particularly in its maritime territory.[[245]](#cite_note-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248) A lack of exploration and decreasing proven reserves have forced Bangladesh to import [LNG](/wiki/LNG "LNG") from abroad.[[247]](#cite_note-249)[[248]](#cite_note-250)[[249]](#cite_note-251) Gas shortages were further exacerbated by the [Russo-Ukrainian war](/wiki/Russo-Ukrainian_war_(2022%E2%80%93present) "Russo-Ukrainian war (2022–present)").[[250]](#cite_note-252) Bangladesh stopped buying [spot price](/wiki/Spot_price "Spot price") LNG temporarily in July 2022, despite constant load-shedding, due to a steep price hike in the global market.[[251]](#cite_note-253) It restarted buying spot price LNG once again in February 2023 as prices eased.[[252]](#cite_note-254)

While [government-owned companies in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_government-owned_companies_of_Bangladesh "List of government-owned companies of Bangladesh") generate nearly half of Bangladesh's electricity, privately owned companies like the Summit Group and [Orion Group](/wiki/Orion_Group_(Bangladesh) "Orion Group (Bangladesh)") are playing an increasingly important role in both generating electricity, and supplying machinery, reactors, and equipment.[[253]](#cite_note-255) Bangladesh increased electricity production capacity from 5 gigawatts in 2009 to 25.5 gigawatts in 2022. It plans to further increase it to 50 [gigawatts](/wiki/Gigawatts "Gigawatts") by 2041. US companies like [Chevron](/wiki/Chevron_Corporation "Chevron Corporation") and [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") supply around 55% of Bangladesh's domestic natural gas production and are among the largest investors in power projects. 80% of Bangladesh's installed gas-fired power generation capacity comes from turbines manufactured in the United States.[[254]](#cite_note-256)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Bangladesh](/wiki/Tourism_in_Bangladesh "Tourism in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Save_the_sundarbans_20.jpg/250px-Save_the_sundarbans_20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Save_the_sundarbans_20.jpg)

The [Sundarbans](/wiki/Sundarbans "Sundarbans") is the largest [mangrove forest](/wiki/Mangrove_forest "Mangrove forest") in the world.

The [tourism industry](/wiki/Tourism_in_Bangladesh "Tourism in Bangladesh") is expanding, contributing some 3.02% of total GDP.[[255]](#cite_note-257) Bangladesh's international tourism receipts in 2019 amounted to $391 million.[[256]](#cite_note-258) The country has [three UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Bangladesh "List of World Heritage Sites in Bangladesh") ([the Mosque City](/wiki/Historic_Mosque_City_of_Bagerhat "Historic Mosque City of Bagerhat"), [the Buddhist Vihara](/wiki/Ruins_of_the_Buddhist_Vihara_at_Paharpur "Ruins of the Buddhist Vihara at Paharpur") and the [Sundarbans](/wiki/Sundarbans "Sundarbans")) and seven [tentative-list](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site#Nominating_process "World Heritage Site") sites.[[257]](#cite_note-259) The [World Travel and Tourism Council](/wiki/World_Travel_and_Tourism_Council "World Travel and Tourism Council") (WTTC) reported in 2019 that the travel and tourism industry in Bangladesh directly generated 1,180,500 jobs in 2018 or 1.9% of the country's total employment.[[258]](#cite_note-2019_report-260) According to the same report, Bangladesh experiences around 125,000 international tourist arrivals per year.[[258]](#cite_note-2019_report-260) Domestic spending generated 97.7 per cent of direct travel and tourism gross domestic product (GDP) in 2012.[[259]](#cite_note-2013_report-261)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Bangladesh](/wiki/Demographics_of_Bangladesh "Demographics of Bangladesh")

Further information: [Bengalis](/wiki/Bengalis "Bengalis")

Population (millions)

| Year | Pop. | ±% p.a. |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1960 | 51,828,660 | — |
| 1971 | 70,276,758 | +2.81% |
| 1980 | 88,016,432 | +2.53% |
| 1990 | 111,633,717 | +2.41% |

Bangladesh had a recorded population of 169.8 million in the [2022 census](/wiki/2022_Bangladeshi_census "2022 Bangladeshi census"),[[10]](#cite_note-populationcensus2022-10) which rose to 171.4 million as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit).[[9]](#cite_note-unpop-9) It is the [eighth-most-populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population "List of countries by population") in the world, the [fifth-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_Asian_countries_by_population "List of Asian countries by population") in Asia, and the [most densely populated large country](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density") in the world, with a headline population density of 1,265 people/km2 as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit).[[261]](#cite_note-263) Bangladesh's [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR), once among the highest in the world, has experienced a dramatic decline, from 5.5 in 1985 to 3.7 in 1995, down to 1.9 in 2022,[[262]](#cite_note-264) which is below the [sub-replacement fertility](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") of 2.1.[[263]](#cite_note-265) Most of the population live in rural areas, with only 40% of the population living in urban areas as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit).[[264]](#cite_note-266) Bangladesh has a [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of roughly 28 years, with 26% of the total population aged 14 or younger,[[265]](#cite_note-267) and merely 6% aged 65 and above as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit).[[266]](#cite_note-268)

Bangladesh is an [ethnically and culturally homogeneous society](/wiki/List_of_countries_ranked_by_ethnic_and_cultural_diversity_level "List of countries ranked by ethnic and cultural diversity level"), as [Bengalis](/wiki/Bengali_people "Bengali people") form 99% of the population.[[8]](#cite_note-cia-8) The [Adivasi](/wiki/Adivasi "Adivasi") population includes the [Chakmas](/wiki/Chakma_people "Chakma people"), [Marmas](/wiki/Marma_people "Marma people"), [Santhals](/wiki/Santhal_people "Santhal people"), [Mros](/wiki/Mru_people_(Mrucha) "Mru people (Mrucha)"), [Tanchangyas](/wiki/Tanchangya_people "Tanchangya people"), [Bawms](/wiki/Bawm_people "Bawm people"), [Tripuris](/wiki/Tripuri_people "Tripuri people"), [Khasis](/wiki/Khasi_people "Khasi people"), [Khumis](/wiki/Khumi_people "Khumi people"), [Kukis](/wiki/Kuki_people "Kuki people"), [Garos](/wiki/Garo_people "Garo people"), and [Bisnupriya Manipuris](/wiki/Bisnupriya_Manipuri_people "Bisnupriya Manipuri people"). The Chittagong Hill Tracts region experienced unrest and an [insurgency](/wiki/Chittagong_Hill_Tracts_conflict "Chittagong Hill Tracts conflict") from 1975 to 1997 in an autonomy movement by its indigenous people. Although a peace accord was signed in 1997, the region [remains militarised](/wiki/Operation_Uttaran "Operation Uttaran").[[267]](#cite_note-rashiduzzaman-269) [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu")-speaking [stranded Pakistanis](/wiki/Stranded_Pakistanis "Stranded Pakistanis") were given citizenship by the Supreme Court in 2008.[[268]](#cite_note-270) Bangladesh also hosts over 700,000 Rohingya refugees since 2017, giving it one of the largest refugee populations in the world.[[151]](#cite_note-RohingyaHRW-153)

### Urban centres

Further information: [List of cities and towns in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Bangladesh "List of cities and towns in Bangladesh")

Bangladesh's capital Dhaka and the largest city and is overseen by two city corporations that manage between them the northern and southern parts of the city. There are 13 [city corporations](/wiki/List_of_City_Corporations_of_Bangladesh "List of City Corporations of Bangladesh") which hold mayoral elections: Dhaka South, Dhaka North, [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong"), [Comilla](/wiki/Comilla "Comilla"), [Khulna](/wiki/Khulna "Khulna"), [Mymensingh](/wiki/Mymensingh "Mymensingh"), [Sylhet](/wiki/Sylhet "Sylhet"), [Rajshahi](/wiki/Rajshahi "Rajshahi"), [Barisal](/wiki/Barisal "Barisal"), [Rangpur](/wiki/Rangpur,_Bangladesh "Rangpur, Bangladesh"), [Gazipur](/wiki/Gazipur,_Dhaka_Division "Gazipur, Dhaka Division"), [Bogura](/wiki/Bogura "Bogura") and [Narayanganj](/wiki/Narayanganj "Narayanganj"). Mayors are elected for five-year terms. Altogether there are 506 urban centres in Bangladesh which 43 cities have a population of more than 100,000.
