### Language

Main article: [Languages of Bangladesh](/wiki/Languages_of_Bangladesh "Languages of Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Shaheed_Minar%2C_Dhaka%2C_Bangladesh.jpg/250px-Shaheed_Minar%2C_Dhaka%2C_Bangladesh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shaheed_Minar,_Dhaka,_Bangladesh.jpg)

The [Shaheed Minar](/wiki/Shaheed_Minar,_Dhaka "Shaheed Minar, Dhaka"), a national monument in Dhaka established to commemorate the martyrs of the 1952 [Bengali language movement](/wiki/Bengali_language_movement "Bengali language movement"), is a symbol of Bengali nationalism.

The official and predominant language of Bangladesh is [Bengali](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language"), which is spoken by more than 99% of the population as their [native language](/wiki/First_language "First language").[[269]](#cite_note-271)[[270]](#cite_note-LOG-272) Bengali is described as a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") where there are various [dialects](/wiki/Bengali_dialects "Bengali dialects") spoken throughout the country. There is a [diglossia](/wiki/Diglossia "Diglossia") in which much of the population can understand or speak in Standard Colloquial Bengali, and in their regional dialect or language variety.[[271]](#cite_note-273) These include [Chittagonian](/wiki/Chittagonian_language "Chittagonian language") which is spoken in the southeastern region of Chittagong,[[272]](#cite_note-274) [Noakhali](/wiki/Noakhali_language "Noakhali language") spoken in the southern district of [Noakhali](/wiki/Noakhali_District "Noakhali District")[[273]](#cite_note-275)[[274]](#cite_note-276) and [Sylheti](/wiki/Sylheti_language "Sylheti language") spoken in the northeastern region of [Sylhet](/wiki/Sylhet_Division "Sylhet Division").[[270]](#cite_note-LOG-272)

English plays an important role in Bangladesh's judicial and educational affairs, due to the country's history as part of the British Empire. It is widely spoken and commonly understood, and is taught as a compulsory subject in all [schools](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Bangladesh "List of schools in Bangladesh"), [colleges](/wiki/List_of_colleges_in_Bangladesh "List of colleges in Bangladesh") and [universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Bangladesh "List of universities in Bangladesh"), while the English-medium educational system is widely attended.[[275]](#cite_note-277)

Tribal languages, although increasingly endangered, include the [Chakma language](/wiki/Chakma_language "Chakma language"), another native Eastern Indo-Aryan language, spoken by the Chakma people.[[276]](#cite_note-278) Others are [Garo](/wiki/Garo_language "Garo language"), [Meitei](/wiki/Meitei_language "Meitei language"), [Kokborok](/wiki/Kokborok "Kokborok") and [Rakhine](/wiki/Rakhine_language "Rakhine language"). Among the [Austroasiatic languages](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages"), the most spoken is the [Santali language](/wiki/Santali_language "Santali language"), native to the [Santal people](/wiki/Santal_people "Santal people").[[277]](#cite_note-279) The [stranded Pakistanis](/wiki/Stranded_Pakistanis_in_Bangladesh "Stranded Pakistanis in Bangladesh") and some sections of the [Old Dhakaites](/wiki/Dhakaiyas "Dhakaiyas") often use [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu") as their native tongue. Still, the usage of the latter remains highly reproached.[[278]](#cite_note-280)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Bangladesh](/wiki/Religion_in_Bangladesh "Religion in Bangladesh")

Religions in Bangladesh (2022 census)[[6]](#cite_note-census-6)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Religion | |  | Percent | |
| [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Bangladesh "Islam in Bangladesh") | |  | 91.04% | |
| [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Bangladesh "Hinduism in Bangladesh") | |  | 7.94% | |
| [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Bangladesh "Buddhism in Bangladesh") | |  | 0.60% | |
| [Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Bangladesh "Christianity in Bangladesh") | |  | 0.31% | |
| Others | |  | 0.11% | |

[Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Bangladesh "Islam in Bangladesh") is the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of Bangladesh.[[279]](#cite_note-state_religion-281) However, the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Bangladesh "Constitution of Bangladesh") also upholds secularism and ensures equal rights for all religions.[[280]](#cite_note-secularism-282)[[279]](#cite_note-state_religion-281) Every citizen has the freedom to practice any religion.

[Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Bangladesh "Islam in Bangladesh") the largest religion across the country, being followed by about 91.1% of the population.[[6]](#cite_note-census-6) The vast majority of Bangladeshi citizens are [Bengali Muslims](/wiki/Bengali_Muslims "Bengali Muslims"), adhering to [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam").[[281]](#cite_note-state-283) The country is the third-most populous Muslim-majority state in the world and has the fourth-largest overall Muslim population.[[282]](#cite_note-284)

[Bengali Hindus](/wiki/Bengali_Hindus "Bengali Hindus") form the country's [second-largest religious minority](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Bangladesh "Hinduism in Bangladesh") and the [third-largest Hindu community](/wiki/Hindus#Demographics "Hindus") in the world. According to the [2022 census](/wiki/2022_Bangladeshi_census "2022 Bangladeshi census") Hindus form 7.95% of the total population.[[281]](#cite_note-state-283)[[6]](#cite_note-census-6) In the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_Bangladeshi_census "2011 Bangladeshi census"), Hindus formed 8.54% of the population.

[Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Bangladesh "Buddhism in Bangladesh") is the third-most followed religion, adhered to by merely 0.6% of the population.[[6]](#cite_note-census-6) Bangladeshi Buddhists are concentrated among the tribal ethnic groups in the [Chittagong Hill Tracts](/wiki/Chittagong_Hill_Tracts "Chittagong Hill Tracts") and by the [Bengali Buddhist](/wiki/Bengali_Buddhists "Bengali Buddhists") minority across coastal Chittagong, who mostly follow the [Theravada](/wiki/Theravada "Theravada") school.[[281]](#cite_note-state-283)

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Bangladesh "Christianity in Bangladesh") is the fourth-largest religion at 0.3%, followed mainly by a small [Bengali Christian](/wiki/Bengali_Christians "Bengali Christians") minority.[[6]](#cite_note-census-6) 0.1% of the population practices other religions such as [Animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism") or is [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion").[[281]](#cite_note-state-283)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Bangladesh](/wiki/Education_in_Bangladesh "Education in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Bangladesh_Literacy_Rate_%282022%2C_Total%29.svg/250px-Bangladesh_Literacy_Rate_%282022%2C_Total%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bangladesh_Literacy_Rate_(2022,_Total).svg)

The [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") of the districts of Bangladesh

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Curzon_Hall_-_Northern_Facade_-_University_of_Dhaka_-_Dhaka_2015-05-31_1992.JPG/250px-Curzon_Hall_-_Northern_Facade_-_University_of_Dhaka_-_Dhaka_2015-05-31_1992.JPG)](/wiki/File:Curzon_Hall_-_Northern_Facade_-_University_of_Dhaka_-_Dhaka_2015-05-31_1992.JPG)

[Curzon Hall](/wiki/Curzon_Hall "Curzon Hall") of the [University of Dhaka](/wiki/University_of_Dhaka "University of Dhaka")

The constitution states that all children shall receive free and compulsory education.[[283]](#cite_note-285) Education in Bangladesh is overseen by the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Bangladesh) "Ministry of Education (Bangladesh)"). The [Ministry of Primary and Mass Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Primary_and_Mass_Education "Ministry of Primary and Mass Education") is responsible for implementing policy for primary education and state-funded schools at a local level. Primary and secondary education is [compulsory](/wiki/Compulsory_education "Compulsory education"), and is financed by the state and free of charge in public schools. Bangladesh has a [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") of 76% as of 2021: 79% for males and 71.9% for females.[[284]](#cite_note-286)[[285]](#cite_note-287)[[286]](#cite_note-288) Its educational system is three-tiered and heavily subsidised, with the government operating many schools at the primary, secondary and higher secondary levels and subsidising many private schools. However, government expenditure in education remains among the lowest in the world, at only 1.8% of the total GDP.[[287]](#cite_note-289)

The education system is divided into five levels: primary (first to fifth grade), junior secondary (sixth to eighth grade), secondary (ninth and tenth grade), higher secondary (11th and 12th grade), and tertiary which is university level.[[288]](#cite_note-CompEd2-290) Primary level students have to pass the [Primary Education Completion](/wiki/Primary_Education_Completion "Primary Education Completion") (PEC) exam to proceed to junior secondary. The junior secondary students then give the [Junior School Certificate](/wiki/Junior_School_Certificate "Junior School Certificate") (JSC) exam to get enrolled in ninth grade, while tenth-grade students have to pass the [Secondary School Certificate](/wiki/Secondary_School_Certificate_(Bangladesh) "Secondary School Certificate (Bangladesh)") (SSC) exam to proceed to eleventh grade. Lastly, students have to pass the [Higher Secondary Certificate](/wiki/Higher_Secondary_Certificate "Higher Secondary Certificate") (HSC) exam at grade twelve to apply for higher education or universities.[[289]](#cite_note-291)

[Universities](/wiki/Universities_in_Bangladesh "Universities in Bangladesh") are three general types: public (government-owned and funded by the [University Grants Commission](/wiki/University_Grants_Commission_(Bangladesh) "University Grants Commission (Bangladesh)")), private (privately owned universities) and international (operated and funded by international organisations). The country has 55 public,[[290]](#cite_note-292) 115 private[[291]](#cite_note-293) and 2 international universities.[[292]](#cite_note-294) [National University](/wiki/National_University,_Bangladesh "National University, Bangladesh") is the [second-largest university in the world by enrolment](/wiki/List_of_largest_universities_and_university_networks_by_enrollment "List of largest universities and university networks by enrollment").[[293]](#cite_note-295) The [University of Dhaka](/wiki/University_of_Dhaka "University of Dhaka"), established in 1921, is the oldest public university.[[294]](#cite_note-296) [BUET](/wiki/Bangladesh_University_of_Engineering_and_Technology "Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology") is the premiere university for [engineering education](/wiki/Engineering_education "Engineering education"). The [University of Chittagong](/wiki/University_of_Chittagong "University of Chittagong"), established in 1966, has the largest campus.[[295]](#cite_note-297) [BUP](/wiki/Bangladesh_University_of_Professionals "Bangladesh University of Professionals") is the largest public university affiliated with the armed forces.[[296]](#cite_note-298) [Dhaka College](/wiki/Dhaka_College "Dhaka College"), established in 1841, is among the oldest educational institutes in the Indian subcontinent.[[297]](#cite_note-299) Medical education is provided by 39 government, 6 armed force and 68 private [medical colleges](/wiki/List_of_medical_colleges_in_Bangladesh "List of medical colleges in Bangladesh"). All medical colleges are affiliated with the [Ministry of Health and Family Welfare](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_and_Family_Welfare_(Bangladesh) "Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (Bangladesh)").[[298]](#cite_note-300) [North South University](/wiki/North_South_University "North South University") is often viewed as the leading private University of the nation.[[299]](#cite_note-301)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Bangladesh](/wiki/Health_in_Bangladesh "Health in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Life_expectancy_by_WBG_-Bangladesh.png/250px-Life_expectancy_by_WBG_-Bangladesh.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_by_WBG_-Bangladesh.png)

Historical development of [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") in Bangladesh, displaying significant strides since independence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Child_mortality_rate_in_Bangladesh.png/250px-Child_mortality_rate_in_Bangladesh.png)](/wiki/File:Child_mortality_rate_in_Bangladesh.png)

Historical development of [child mortality](/wiki/Child_mortality "Child mortality") in Bangladesh since 1990, showing a significant decrease

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/A_community_health_worker_conducting_a_survey_in_the_Korail_slum%2C_Bangladesh_%288630810827%29.jpg/250px-A_community_health_worker_conducting_a_survey_in_the_Korail_slum%2C_Bangladesh_%288630810827%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_community_health_worker_conducting_a_survey_in_the_Korail_slum,_Bangladesh_(8630810827).jpg)

A community health worker conducting a survey in a slum of the capital city, Dhaka

Bangladesh, by the constitution, guarantees healthcare services as a fundamental right to all of its citizens.[[300]](#cite_note-302) The [Ministry of Health and Family Welfare](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_and_Family_Welfare_(Bangladesh) "Ministry of Health and Family Welfare (Bangladesh)") is the largest institutional healthcare provider in Bangladesh,[[301]](#cite_note-overview-303) and contains two divisions: [Health Service Division](/wiki/Health_Service_Division "Health Service Division") and [Medical Education And Family Welfare Division](/wiki/Medical_Education_And_Family_Welfare_Division "Medical Education And Family Welfare Division").[[302]](#cite_note-304) However, healthcare facilities in Bangladesh are considered less than adequate, although they have improved as the economy has grown and poverty levels have decreased significantly.[[301]](#cite_note-overview-303) Bangladesh faces a severe health workforce crisis, as formally trained providers make up a small percentage of the total health workforce.[[303]](#cite_note-305) Significant deficiencies in the treatment practices of village doctors persist, with widespread harmful and inappropriate drug prescribing.[[304]](#cite_note-306)

Bangladesh's poor healthcare system suffers from severe underfunding from the government.[[301]](#cite_note-overview-303) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit), some 2.36% of total GDP was attributed to healthcare,[[305]](#cite_note-307) and domestic general government spending on healthcare was 16.88% of the total budget,[[306]](#cite_note-308) while out-of-pocket expenditures made up the vast majority of the total budget, totalling roughly 73%.[[307]](#cite_note-309) Domestic private health expenditure was about 75.48% of the total healthcare expenditure.[[308]](#cite_note-310) There were only 5.3 doctors per 10,000 people, and about six physicians[[309]](#cite_note-311) and six nurses per 1,000 people,[[310]](#cite_note-312) while the number of hospital beds is 9 per 1,000.[[311]](#cite_note-313)[[312]](#cite_note-314) The specialist surgical workforce was only 3 per 100,000 people,[[313]](#cite_note-315) and there were about 5 community health workers per 1,000 people.[[314]](#cite_note-316)

Roughly 60% of the population had access to [drinking water](/wiki/Drinking_water "Drinking water") in 2022.[[315]](#cite_note-317) In 2002, it was estimated that half of the drinking water was polluted with [arsenic](/wiki/Arsenic "Arsenic"), exceeding levels of 10 micrograms per litre.[[316]](#cite_note-318) Bangladesh is crippled with one of the worst air qualities in the world, mostly concentrated in the densely populated urban areas, especially the capital [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka") and its [metropolitan area](/wiki/Greater_Dhaka "Greater Dhaka").[[317]](#cite_note-319) The World Bank estimated that roughly 80,000–90,000 deaths occurred in Bangladesh due to the drastic effects of air pollution in 2019. It was second-leading cause of death and disability, costing the country roughly 4–4.4% per cent of its total GDP.[[318]](#cite_note-320)

As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit), the overall life expectancy in Bangladesh at birth was 74 years (72 years for males and 76 years for females).[[319]](#cite_note-321) It has a comparably high [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") rate (24 per 1,000 live births) and [child mortality](/wiki/Child_mortality "Child mortality") rate (29 per 1,000 live births).[[320]](#cite_note-322)[[321]](#cite_note-323) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Bangladesh&action=edit), [maternal mortality](/wiki/Maternal_death "Maternal death") remains high, clocking at 123 per 100,000 live births.[[322]](#cite_note-324) Bangladesh is a key source market for [medical tourism](/wiki/Medical_tourism "Medical tourism") for various countries, mainly [India](/wiki/Medical_tourism_in_India "Medical tourism in India"),[[323]](#cite_note-325) due to its citizens dissatisfaction and distrust over their own healthcare system.[[324]](#cite_note-326)

The main causes of death are [coronary artery disease](/wiki/Coronary_artery_disease "Coronary artery disease"), stroke, and chronic [respiratory disease](/wiki/Respiratory_disease "Respiratory disease"); comprising 62% and 60% of all adult male and female deaths, respectively.[[325]](#cite_note-327) [Malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition") is a major and persistent problem in Bangladesh, mainly affecting the rural regions, more than half of the population suffers from it. Severe acute malnutrition affects 450,000 children, while nearly 2 million children have moderate acute malnutrition. For children under the age of five, 52% are affected by [anaemia](/wiki/Anaemia "Anaemia"), 41% are [stunted](/wiki/Stunted_growth "Stunted growth"), 16% are [wasted](/wiki/Wasting "Wasting"), and 36% are [underweight](/wiki/Underweight "Underweight"). A quarter of women are underweight and around 15% have short stature, while over half also suffer from anaemia.[[326]](#cite_note-328) Bangladesh was ranked 84th out of the 127 countries listed in the 2024 [Global Hunger Index](/wiki/Global_Hunger_Index "Global Hunger Index").[[327]](#cite_note-329)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Bangladesh](/wiki/Culture_of_Bangladesh "Culture of Bangladesh")

Further information: [Culture of Bengal](/wiki/Culture_of_Bengal "Culture of Bengal"), [Museums in Bangladesh](/wiki/Museums_in_Bangladesh "Museums in Bangladesh"), and [List of libraries in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_libraries_in_Bangladesh "List of libraries in Bangladesh")

### Holidays and festivals

Main articles: [Public holidays in Bangladesh](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Bangladesh "Public holidays in Bangladesh") and [List of festivals in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_Bangladesh "List of festivals in Bangladesh")

Traditional festivals include [Pahela Baishakh](/wiki/Pahela_Baishakh "Pahela Baishakh") (Bengali New Year), which is the major festival of Bengali culture; with widespread festivities.[[328]](#cite_note-330) [Pohela Falgun](/wiki/Pohela_Falgun "Pohela Falgun") coincides with [Valentine's Day](/wiki/Valentine%27s_Day_in_Bangladesh "Valentine's Day in Bangladesh"), and is celebrated with a display of music, dance and other cultural activities.[[329]](#cite_note-331) Other festivals include [Nabonno](/wiki/Nabanna "Nabanna") and Poush Parbon, which celebrate new harvests of crops.[[330]](#cite_note-332) [Shakrain](/wiki/Shakrain "Shakrain") is an annual celebration, observed by flying [kites](/wiki/Kite "Kite"), occurring at the end of [Poush](/wiki/Poush "Poush"), the ninth month of the [Bengali calendar](/wiki/Bengali_calendar "Bengali calendar"). The festival coincides with [Makar Sankranti](/wiki/Makar_Sankranti "Makar Sankranti") celebrated in India and Nepal.[[331]](#cite_note-333)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Jatiyo_Sritisoudho.jpg/250px-Jatiyo_Sritisoudho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jatiyo_Sritisoudho.jpg)

Victory Day celebration at [National Martyrs' Memorial](/wiki/National_Martyrs%27_Memorial "National Martyrs' Memorial")

Among religious festivals, the two biggest festivals of the Muslim majority are [Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr"), which marks the end of the month of [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan")—and [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha"), which is the festival of [sacrifice](/wiki/Sacrifice#Islam "Sacrifice").[[332]](#cite_note-334) Both Eids are celebrated with the longest streak of [national holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Bangladesh "Public holidays in Bangladesh").[[333]](#cite_note-335) Other Muslim festivals include [Mawlid](/wiki/Mawlid "Mawlid") (Eid-e-Milad Un Nabi),[[334]](#cite_note-336) [Ashura](/wiki/Ashura "Ashura") on the tenth day of [Muharram](/wiki/Muharram "Muharram"),[[335]](#cite_note-337) [Chaand Raat](/wiki/Chaand_Raat "Chaand Raat"),[[336]](#cite_note-338) and [Shab-e-Barat](/wiki/Shab-e-Barat "Shab-e-Barat"), [Shab-e-Meraj](/wiki/Shab-e-Meraj "Shab-e-Meraj"), [Shab-e-Qadr](/wiki/Night_of_Power "Night of Power") during [Ramadan](/wiki/Ramadan "Ramadan") and [Bishwa Ijtema](/wiki/Bishwa_Ijtema "Bishwa Ijtema").[[337]](#cite_note-339) The most celebrated Hindu festival is [Durga Puja](/wiki/Durga_Puja "Durga Puja").[[338]](#cite_note-340) Other major Hindu festivals include [Krishna Janmashtami](/wiki/Krishna_Janmashtami "Krishna Janmashtami"), [Kali Puja](/wiki/Kali_Puja "Kali Puja"), [Saraswati Puja](/wiki/Saraswati_Puja "Saraswati Puja"), and [Ratha Yatra](/wiki/Ratha_Yatra "Ratha Yatra").[[339]](#cite_note-341)[[340]](#cite_note-342) The biggest festival of the Buddhists across the country is [Buddha Purnima](/wiki/Vesak "Vesak"), which marks the birth of [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha").[[341]](#cite_note-343) Among Christians, Christmas, known as [Boro Din](/wiki/Boro_Din "Boro Din"), and [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") are the most widely celebrated .[[342]](#cite_note-344)

Patriotic national festivals include the [Language Movement Day](/wiki/Language_Movement_Day "Language Movement Day"), which is celebrated on 21 February in remembrance of the martyrs of the 1952 [Bengali language movement](/wiki/Bengali_language_movement "Bengali language movement").[[343]](#cite_note-345) It was declared as [International Mother Language Day](/wiki/International_Mother_Language_Day "International Mother Language Day") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 1999.[[344]](#cite_note-346) [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Bangladesh) "Independence Day (Bangladesh)") is celebrated on 26 March to commemorate the [proclamation of independence](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Bangladeshi_Independence "Proclamation of Bangladeshi Independence") from Pakistan.[[345]](#cite_note-star2-347) [Victory Day](/wiki/Victory_Day_(Bangladesh) "Victory Day (Bangladesh)") is celebrated on 16 December to celebrate the victory in the [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War").[[345]](#cite_note-star2-347) Public gatherings are observed at the [Shaheed Minar](/wiki/Shaheed_Minar,_Dhaka "Shaheed Minar, Dhaka") and [National Martyrs' Memorial](/wiki/National_Martyrs%27_Memorial "National Martyrs' Memorial") during the three latter festivals to pay homage to the fallen martyrs.[[346]](#cite_note-348)[[347]](#cite_note-349)

### Literature

Main article: [Bengali literature](/wiki/Bengali_literature "Bengali literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Nazrul.jpg/250px-Nazrul.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nazrul.jpg)

[Kazi Nazrul Islam](/wiki/Kazi_Nazrul_Islam "Kazi Nazrul Islam") in Chittagong, c. 1926, the [national poet](/wiki/National_poet "National poet") of Bangladesh.[[348]](#cite_note-350)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Humayun_ahmed_gossip.jpg/250px-Humayun_ahmed_gossip.jpg)](/wiki/File:Humayun_ahmed_gossip.jpg)

[Humayun Ahmed](/wiki/Humayun_Ahmed "Humayun Ahmed") (left), a popular author and filmmaker

Bengali literature forms an important part of Bengali culture. The [Charyapada](/wiki/Charyapada "Charyapada") poems dating back to the 10th to 12th centuries are the oldest extant examples of the Bengali language.[[349]](#cite_note-351): 40 During the [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate"), medieval Bengali writers were influenced by Arabic and [Persian literature](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature").[[350]](#cite_note-352) Milestones of the medieval age include the [Mangal-Kāvyas](/wiki/Mangal-K%C4%81vya "Mangal-Kāvya").[[351]](#cite_note-run-353) The [Vaishnava Padavali](/wiki/Vaishnava_Padavali "Vaishnava Padavali") movement was led by writers such as [Vidyapati](/wiki/Vidyapati "Vidyapati"), [Chandidas](/wiki/Chandidas "Chandidas"), [Govindadas](/wiki/Govindadas "Govindadas") and [Balarama Dasa](/wiki/Balarama_Dasa "Balarama Dasa"). *[Shreekrishna Kirtana](/wiki/Shreekrishna_Kirtana "Shreekrishna Kirtana")* written by Chandidas marked a particular height of poetic achievement since the Charyapadas.[[351]](#cite_note-run-353) Other important works include [Krittibas Ojha](/wiki/Krittibas_Ojha "Krittibas Ojha")'s translation of the *[Ramayana](/wiki/Ramayana "Ramayana")*, [Kashiram Das](/wiki/Kashiram_Das "Kashiram Das")' translation of the *[Mahabharata](/wiki/Mahabharata "Mahabharata")*, and [Maladhar Basu](/wiki/Maladhar_Basu "Maladhar Basu")'s translation of the *[Bhagavata](/wiki/Bhagavata "Bhagavata")*.[[351]](#cite_note-run-353) Writers such as [Bipradas Pipilai](/wiki/Bipradas_Pipilai "Bipradas Pipilai"),[[352]](#cite_note-354): 349 [Vijay Gupta](/wiki/Vijay_Gupta_(poet) "Vijay Gupta (poet)"),[[353]](#cite_note-355): 104 [Shah Muhammad Sagir](/wiki/Shah_Muhammad_Sagir "Shah Muhammad Sagir"), [Zainuddin](/wiki/Zainuddin_(poet) "Zainuddin (poet)") and [Abdul Hakim](/wiki/Abdul_Hakim_(poet) "Abdul Hakim (poet)") were important figures.[[351]](#cite_note-run-353) [Alaol](/wiki/Alaol "Alaol"), considered a [bard](/wiki/Bard "Bard"), is a prolific poet of medieval period.[[351]](#cite_note-run-353)

The [Bengal Renaissance](/wiki/Bengal_Renaissance "Bengal Renaissance") from the late 18th century to the early 20th century had a profound effect on modern Bengali literature. [Michael Madhusudan Dutt](/wiki/Michael_Madhusudan_Dutt "Michael Madhusudan Dutt") invented the [blank verse](/wiki/Blank_verse "Blank verse") in Bengali literature.[[354]](#cite_note-356) [Mir Mosharraf Hossain](/wiki/Mir_Mosharraf_Hossain "Mir Mosharraf Hossain") was the first prominent Bengali Muslim writer.[[355]](#cite_note-357) [Lalon](/wiki/Lalon "Lalon"), a [fakir](/wiki/Fakir "Fakir") practising [Sufism](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") and [sādhanā](/wiki/S%C4%81dhan%C4%81 "Sādhanā") influenced the [bauls](/wiki/Baul "Baul").[[356]](#cite_note-358) [Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay](/wiki/Sarat_Chandra_Chattopadhyay "Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay") wrote about the characteristics of the Bengali society.[[357]](#cite_note-359)

[Rabindranath Tagore](/wiki/Rabindranath_Tagore "Rabindranath Tagore") was the first Asian and non-European laureate of the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature").[[358]](#cite_note-360) [Kazi Nazrul Islam](/wiki/Kazi_Nazrul_Islam "Kazi Nazrul Islam") was a revolutionary poet who espoused political rebellion against colonialism and fascism.[[359]](#cite_note-361) [Jibanananda Das](/wiki/Jibanananda_Das "Jibanananda Das") was the most recognised Bengali poet after Tagore and Nazrul.[[360]](#cite_note-362) [Begum Rokeya](/wiki/Begum_Rokeya "Begum Rokeya") is regarded as the pioneer feminist writer of Bangladesh.[[361]](#cite_note-363) [Syed Mujtaba Ali](/wiki/Syed_Mujtaba_Ali "Syed Mujtaba Ali") is noted for his [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") views.[[362]](#cite_note-364) [Jasimuddin](/wiki/Jasimuddin "Jasimuddin") was a renowned pastoral poet, popularly called *Palli Kabi* (folk poet).[[363]](#cite_note-365) [Farrukh Ahmad](/wiki/Farrukh_Ahmad "Farrukh Ahmad") is considered the poet of the "Islamic Renaissance".[[364]](#cite_note-366) [Syed Waliullah](/wiki/Syed_Waliullah "Syed Waliullah") was a notable novelist.[[365]](#cite_note-pied-367)

[Shamsur Rahman](/wiki/Shamsur_Rahman_(poet) "Shamsur Rahman (poet)") and [Al Mahmud](/wiki/Al_Mahmud "Al Mahmud") are considered two of the greatest Bangladeshi poets to have emerged in the late 20th century. [Ahmed Sofa](/wiki/Ahmed_Sofa "Ahmed Sofa") is regarded as the most important intellectual in the post-independence era.[[366]](#cite_note-368) [Sufia Kamal](/wiki/Sufia_Kamal "Sufia Kamal") was a major feminist writer.[[367]](#cite_note-369) [Humayun Ahmed](/wiki/Humayun_Ahmed "Humayun Ahmed") was the most popular author in post-Independence Bangladesh.[[365]](#cite_note-pied-367) [Shahidul Zahir](/wiki/Shahidul_Zahir "Shahidul Zahir") was widely acclaimed for his usage of [magical realism](/wiki/Magical_realism "Magical realism").[[368]](#cite_note-370) Other major writers include [Akhteruzzaman Elias](/wiki/Akhteruzzaman_Elias "Akhteruzzaman Elias"), [Shawkat Osman](/wiki/Shawkat_Osman "Shawkat Osman") and [Syed Shamsul Haq](/wiki/Syed_Shamsul_Haq "Syed Shamsul Haq").[[365]](#cite_note-pied-367) [Selina Hossain](/wiki/Selina_Hossain "Selina Hossain") is a prolific female author in the modern era.[[369]](#cite_note-371) [Muhammad Zafar Iqbal](/wiki/Muhammad_Zafar_Iqbal "Muhammad Zafar Iqbal") is a pioneer science fiction writer.[[370]](#cite_note-372) [Anisul Hoque](/wiki/Anisul_Hoque "Anisul Hoque") is a popular contemporary literary figure.[[371]](#cite_note-373) The annual [Ekushey Book Fair](/wiki/Ekushey_Book_Fair "Ekushey Book Fair") and [Dhaka Lit Fest](/wiki/Dhaka_Lit_Fest "Dhaka Lit Fest"), organised by the [Bangla Academy](/wiki/Bangla_Academy "Bangla Academy"), are among the largest literary festivals in South Asia.[[372]](#cite_note-374)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Bangladesh](/wiki/Architecture_of_Bangladesh "Architecture of Bangladesh")

Further information: [Architecture of Bengal](/wiki/Architecture_of_Bengal "Architecture of Bengal") and [List of archaeological sites in Bangladesh](/wiki/List_of_archaeological_sites_in_Bangladesh "List of archaeological sites in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Paharpur_Buddhist_Bihar.jpg/250px-Paharpur_Buddhist_Bihar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paharpur_Buddhist_Bihar.jpg)

[Somapura Mahavihara](/wiki/Somapura_Mahavihara "Somapura Mahavihara") in [Naogaon](/wiki/Naogaon "Naogaon"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Bangladesh "List of World Heritage Sites in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Sixty_Dome_Mosque%2CBagerhat.jpg/250px-Sixty_Dome_Mosque%2CBagerhat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sixty_Dome_Mosque,Bagerhat.jpg)

The [Sixty Dome Mosque](/wiki/Sixty_Dome_Mosque "Sixty Dome Mosque") in [Bagerhat](/wiki/Bagerhat "Bagerhat"), a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Bangladesh "List of World Heritage Sites in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Ahsan_Manzil-Front_View.jpg/250px-Ahsan_Manzil-Front_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ahsan_Manzil-Front_View.jpg)

The [Ahsan Manzil](/wiki/Ahsan_Manzil "Ahsan Manzil") in [Old Dhaka](/wiki/Old_Dhaka "Old Dhaka")

The architecture of Bangladesh is intertwined with that of the [Bengal region](/wiki/Architecture_of_Bengal "Architecture of Bengal") and the broader [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Architecture_of_India "Architecture of India"). It is influenced by the country's [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Bangladesh "Culture of Bangladesh"), [religion](/wiki/Religion_in_Bangladesh "Religion in Bangladesh") and [history](/wiki/History_of_Bangladesh "History of Bangladesh").[[373]](#cite_note-375) Hindu and Buddhist architectural remnants have been found in [Mahasthangarh](/wiki/Mahasthangarh "Mahasthangarh"), which dates back to the 3rd century BCE.[[374]](#cite_note-re-376): 157 [Nandipada](/wiki/Nandipada "Nandipada") and [Swastika](/wiki/Swastika "Swastika") symbols have been found on stone querns in the [Wari-Bateshwar ruins](/wiki/Wari-Bateshwar_ruins "Wari-Bateshwar ruins"), which indicate the presence of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") in the area during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age")—from 400 to 100 BCE.[[375]](#cite_note-377) The [Somapura Mahavihara](/wiki/Somapura_Mahavihara "Somapura Mahavihara") built under the rule of the Buddhist [Pala Empire](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire") in the 8th century is an outstanding example of the pre-Islamic era.[[376]](#cite_note-378) Other Buddhist [vihāras](/wiki/Vih%C4%81ra "Vihāra") include [Shalban Bihar](/wiki/Shalban_Bihar "Shalban Bihar") in [Mainamati](/wiki/Mainamati "Mainamati") and [Bikrampur Vihara](/wiki/Bikrampur_Vihara "Bikrampur Vihara") in [Bikrampur](/wiki/Bikrampur "Bikrampur").[[374]](#cite_note-re-376): 164–167 Recent excavations have also uncovered new evidence of pre-Islamic smaller temples which served the Hindu, Buddhist and [Jain](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism") populations of the area.[[377]](#cite_note-sat-379) [Indo-Islamic architecture](/wiki/Indo-Islamic_architecture "Indo-Islamic architecture") can be seen from the 13th century, especially in the unique mosque architecture of the [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate"), an example being the [Sixty Dome Mosque](/wiki/Sixty_Dome_Mosque "Sixty Dome Mosque") among others in the [Mosque City of Bagerhat](/wiki/Mosque_City_of_Bagerhat "Mosque City of Bagerhat")—which is a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Bangladesh "List of World Heritage Sites in Bangladesh").[[378]](#cite_note-380)[[377]](#cite_note-sat-379)

[Mughal Bengal](/wiki/Mughal_Bengal "Mughal Bengal") saw the spread of [Mughal architecture](/wiki/Mughal_architecture "Mughal architecture") in the region. Examples in Dhaka include the [Bara Katra](/wiki/Bara_Katra "Bara Katra") and [Choto Katra](/wiki/Choto_Katra "Choto Katra") in Old Dhaka, the [Sat Gambuj Mosque](/wiki/Sat_Gambuj_Mosque "Sat Gambuj Mosque") in [Mohammadpur](/wiki/Mohammadpur_Thana "Mohammadpur Thana") and the [Musa Khan Mosque](/wiki/Musa_Khan_Mosque "Musa Khan Mosque") in [Curzon Hall](/wiki/Curzon_Hall "Curzon Hall").[[379]](#cite_note-381)[[380]](#cite_note-382) Notable Mughal-era forts include the [Lalbagh Fort](/wiki/Lalbagh_Fort "Lalbagh Fort") in [Old Dhaka](/wiki/Old_Dhaka "Old Dhaka"), the [Idrakpur Fort](/wiki/Idrakpur_Fort "Idrakpur Fort") in [Munshiganj](/wiki/Munshiganj "Munshiganj")—and the [Hajiganj Fort](/wiki/Hajiganj_Fort "Hajiganj Fort") and the [Sonakanda Fort](/wiki/Sonakanda_Fort "Sonakanda Fort") in [Narayanganj](/wiki/Narayanganj "Narayanganj"), respectively.[[381]](#cite_note-383) The [Kantajew Temple](/wiki/Kantajew_Temple "Kantajew Temple") and [Dhakeshwari Temple](/wiki/Dhakeshwari_Temple "Dhakeshwari Temple") are excellent examples of late medieval [Hindu temple architecture](/wiki/Hindu_temple_architecture "Hindu temple architecture").[[382]](#cite_note-384)[[383]](#cite_note-385)

Bengali [vernacular architecture](/wiki/Vernacular_architecture "Vernacular architecture") is noted for pioneering the [bungalow](/wiki/Bungalow "Bungalow").[[384]](#cite_note-386) [Panam Nagar](/wiki/Panam_Nagar "Panam Nagar") in [Sonargaon](/wiki/Sonargaon "Sonargaon") exhibits architectural influence from the Sultanate, Mughal, British and hybrid colonial traditions.[[385]](#cite_note-387): 22 [Indo-Saracenic architecture](/wiki/Indo-Saracenic_architecture "Indo-Saracenic architecture") flourished during the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"),[[386]](#cite_note-388) examples include the [Curzon Hall](/wiki/Curzon_Hall "Curzon Hall") of the [University of Dhaka](/wiki/University_of_Dhaka "University of Dhaka"), the [Chittagong Court Building](/wiki/Chittagong_Court_Building "Chittagong Court Building"), [Rangpur Town Hall](/wiki/Rangpur_Town_Hall "Rangpur Town Hall") and [Rajshahi College](/wiki/Rajshahi_College "Rajshahi College"). The [zamindar](/wiki/Zamindar "Zamindar") gentry built many palaces in the latter style, including the [Ahsan Manzil](/wiki/Ahsan_Manzil "Ahsan Manzil"), the [Baliati Zamnidar Bari](/wiki/Baliati_Zamindari "Baliati Zamindari"), the [Tajhat Palace](/wiki/Tajhat_Palace "Tajhat Palace"), the [Rose Garden Palace](/wiki/Rose_Garden_Palace "Rose Garden Palace"), the [Dighapatia Palace](/wiki/Uttara_Gonobhaban "Uttara Gonobhaban"), the [Puthia Rajbari](/wiki/Puthia_Rajbari "Puthia Rajbari"), [Natore Rajbari](/wiki/Natore_Rajbari "Natore Rajbari") and the [Mohera Zamindar Bari](/wiki/Mohera_Zamindar_Bari "Mohera Zamindar Bari"). [Muzharul Islam](/wiki/Muzharul_Islam "Muzharul Islam") is considered to be a pioneer of [modernist movement](/wiki/Modern_architecture "Modern architecture") in Bangladesh and South Asia.[[387]](#cite_note-389) [Louis Kahn](/wiki/Louis_Kahn "Louis Kahn") is a notable foreign architect who designed the [National Parliament Building](/wiki/Jatiya_Sangsad_Bhaban "Jatiya Sangsad Bhaban") in [Sher-e-Bangla Nagar](/wiki/Sher-e-Bangla_Nagar "Sher-e-Bangla Nagar").[[388]](#cite_note-390)

### Visual arts, crafts and clothing

Main articles: [Bangladeshi art](/wiki/Bangladeshi_art "Bangladeshi art"), [Sculpture of Bangladesh](/wiki/Sculpture_of_Bangladesh "Sculpture of Bangladesh"), and [Textile industry in Bangladesh](/wiki/Textile_industry_in_Bangladesh "Textile industry in Bangladesh")

Further information: [Muslin trade in Bengal](/wiki/Muslin_trade_in_Bengal "Muslin trade in Bengal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Kantha_%28Quilt%29_LACMA_AC1994.131.1.jpg/250px-Kantha_%28Quilt%29_LACMA_AC1994.131.1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kantha_(Quilt)_LACMA_AC1994.131.1.jpg)

Embroidery on [Nakshi kantha](/wiki/Nakshi_kantha "Nakshi kantha") (embroidered [quilt](/wiki/Quilt "Quilt")), a centuries-old Bengali art tradition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Aparajeyo_Bangla_-_1979_CE_-_Sculpture_by_Syed_Abdullah_Khalid_-_University_of_Dhaka_Campus_-_Dhaka_2015-05-31_2367.JPG/250px-Aparajeyo_Bangla_-_1979_CE_-_Sculpture_by_Syed_Abdullah_Khalid_-_University_of_Dhaka_Campus_-_Dhaka_2015-05-31_2367.JPG)](/wiki/File:Aparajeyo_Bangla_-_1979_CE_-_Sculpture_by_Syed_Abdullah_Khalid_-_University_of_Dhaka_Campus_-_Dhaka_2015-05-31_2367.JPG)

*[Aparajeyo Bangla](/wiki/Aparajeyo_Bangla "Aparajeyo Bangla")*, a sculpture dedicated to the [Bangladesh Liberation War](/wiki/Bangladesh_Liberation_War "Bangladesh Liberation War"), located on the campus of the University of Dhaka

The recorded history of art in Bangladesh can be traced to the 3rd century BCE, when [terracotta](/wiki/Terracotta "Terracotta") sculptures were made in the region. In classical antiquity, notable sculptural [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu_art "Hindu art"), [Jain](/wiki/Jain_art "Jain art"), and [Buddhist art](/wiki/Buddhist_art "Buddhist art") developed in the [Pala Empire](/wiki/Pala_Empire "Pala Empire") and the [Sena dynasty](/wiki/Sena_dynasty "Sena dynasty"). The [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") saw [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art "Islamic art") evolve since the 14th century. During the Mughal rule, [Jamdani](/wiki/Jamdani "Jamdani"), a unique design on fine [muslin](/wiki/Muslin "Muslin"); was woven on Persian [motifs](/wiki/Motif_(textile_arts) "Motif (textile arts)") in Dhaka.[[389]](#cite_note-391): 239 It was classified by UNESCO as an [Intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/Intangible_cultural_heritage "Intangible cultural heritage") in 2013.[[390]](#cite_note-392) Bangladesh also produces the [Rajshahi silk](/wiki/Rajshahi_silk "Rajshahi silk"), a fine silk renowned for its softness and ability to create sophisticated designs.[[391]](#cite_note-393): 158 [Ivory](/wiki/Ivory "Ivory"), [brass](/wiki/Brassware_industry_in_Bangladesh "Brassware industry in Bangladesh") and [pottery](/wiki/Pottery_of_Bangladesh "Pottery of Bangladesh") has deep roots in Bangladeshi culture.[[392]](#cite_note-394): 483–485 The [Nakshi Kantha](/wiki/Nakshi_Kantha "Nakshi Kantha"), a centuries-old [embroidery](/wiki/Embroidery "Embroidery") tradition for [quilts](/wiki/Quilt "Quilt") in Bengal, is made throughout Bangladesh.[[393]](#cite_note-395): 44

The [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") movement in Bangladesh took shape in post-independence [East Bengal](/wiki/East_Bengal "East Bengal"), especially with the pioneering works of [Zainul Abedin](/wiki/Zainul_Abedin "Zainul Abedin").[[394]](#cite_note-star-396) Other leading painters include [SM Sultan](/wiki/SM_Sultan "SM Sultan"), [Mohammad Kibria](/wiki/Mohammad_Kibria "Mohammad Kibria"), [Safiuddin Ahmed](/wiki/Safiuddin_Ahmed "Safiuddin Ahmed"), [Shahabuddin Ahmed](/wiki/Shahabuddin_Ahmed_(artist) "Shahabuddin Ahmed (artist)"), [Kanak Chanpa Chakma](/wiki/Kanak_Chanpa_Chakma "Kanak Chanpa Chakma"),[[395]](#cite_note-397) [Qayyum Chowdhury](/wiki/Qayyum_Chowdhury "Qayyum Chowdhury"), [Rashid Choudhury](/wiki/Rashid_Choudhury "Rashid Choudhury"), [Quamrul Hassan](/wiki/Quamrul_Hassan "Quamrul Hassan"), [Rafiqun Nabi](/wiki/Rafiqun_Nabi "Rafiqun Nabi") and [Syed Jahangir](/wiki/Syed_Jahangir "Syed Jahangir").[[394]](#cite_note-star-396)

[Novera Ahmed](/wiki/Novera_Ahmed "Novera Ahmed") is the pioneer of modernist sculpture in Bangladesh.[[396]](#cite_note-398) Other eminent sculptors include [Nitun Kundu](/wiki/Nitun_Kundu "Nitun Kundu"), [Syed Abdullah Khalid](/wiki/Syed_Abdullah_Khalid "Syed Abdullah Khalid"), [Hamiduzzaman Khan](/wiki/Hamiduzzaman_Khan "Hamiduzzaman Khan"), [Shamim Sikder](/wiki/Shamim_Sikder "Shamim Sikder"), [Ferdousi Priyabhashini](/wiki/Ferdousi_Priyabhashini "Ferdousi Priyabhashini") and [Abdur Razzaque](/wiki/Abdur_Razzaque_(artist) "Abdur Razzaque (artist)").[[397]](#cite_note-399) The annual [Mangal Shobhajatra](/wiki/Mangal_Shobhajatra "Mangal Shobhajatra") (Bengali New Year parade) organised by the [Faculty of Fine Arts](/wiki/Faculty_of_Fine_Arts,_University_of_Dhaka "Faculty of Fine Arts, University of Dhaka") of the University of Dhaka on [Pohela Boishakh](/wiki/Pohela_Boishakh "Pohela Boishakh") was enlisted as an Intangible cultural heritage by UNESCO in 2016.[[398]](#cite_note-400) Photography as a form of art has seen rapid growth in the 21st century. [Chobi Mela](/wiki/Chobi_Mela_International_Photography_Festival "Chobi Mela International Photography Festival"), held biennially, is considered the largest photography festival in Asia.[[399]](#cite_note-401)

[Lungi](/wiki/Lungi "Lungi") is the most common informal clothing for men,[[400]](#cite_note-402) while [kurta](/wiki/Kurta "Kurta") (*panjabi*) and [pajama](/wiki/Pajama "Pajama") are worn by men on festivals and holidays.[[401]](#cite_note-403) Domestically tailored [suits](/wiki/Suit_(clothing) "Suit (clothing)"), [neckties](/wiki/Neckties "Neckties") and [pants](/wiki/Trouser "Trouser") are customarily worn by men at formal events, and the traditional [sherwani](/wiki/Sherwani "Sherwani") and [churidar](/wiki/Churidar "Churidar") are worn along with the [turban](/wiki/Turban "Turban") in weddings.[[402]](#cite_note-arts-404) Women commonly wear the [shalwar kameez](/wiki/Shalwar_kameez "Shalwar kameez") accompanied by [orna](/wiki/Dupatta "Dupatta"); while [sari](/wiki/Sari "Sari") is worn on more formal events.[[402]](#cite_note-arts-404) Some women follow [Islamic clothing](/wiki/Islamic_clothing "Islamic clothing").[[403]](#cite_note-405)

### Performing arts

Main articles: [Music of Bangladesh](/wiki/Music_of_Bangladesh "Music of Bangladesh") and [Dance in Bangladesh](/wiki/Dance_in_Bangladesh "Dance in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Bangladesh_Udichi_Shilpigoshthi_-_Chittagong_University_Sangsad_celebrating_Pohela_Falgun_1423_at_CU_Muktomoncho_%2802%29.jpg/250px-Bangladesh_Udichi_Shilpigoshthi_-_Chittagong_University_Sangsad_celebrating_Pohela_Falgun_1423_at_CU_Muktomoncho_%2802%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangladesh_Udichi_Shilpigoshthi_-_Chittagong_University_Sangsad_celebrating_Pohela_Falgun_1423_at_CU_Muktomoncho_(02).jpg)

Dance in celebration of [Pohela Falgun](/wiki/Pohela_Falgun "Pohela Falgun") at the [University of Chittagong](/wiki/University_of_Chittagong "University of Chittagong")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Ektara_player.jpg/250px-Ektara_player.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ektara_player.jpg)

A [baul](/wiki/Baul "Baul") playing the [ektara](/wiki/Ektara "Ektara") at the shrine of [Lalon](/wiki/Lalon "Lalon") in [Kushtia](/wiki/Kushtia "Kushtia")

[Theatre in Bangladesh](/wiki/Theatre_in_Bangladesh "Theatre in Bangladesh") includes various forms with a history dating back to the 4th century CE.[[404]](#cite_note-Ahmed-406) It includes narrative forms, song and dance forms, supra-personae forms, performances with scroll paintings, puppet theatre and processional forms.[[404]](#cite_note-Ahmed-406) The [Jatra](/wiki/Jatra_(theatre) "Jatra (theatre)") is the most popular form of Bengali folk theatre.[[405]](#cite_note-407) Apart from the various forms of [Indian classical dances](/wiki/Indian_classical_dance "Indian classical dance"), including the *[Kathakali](/wiki/Kathakali "Kathakali")*, *[Bharatanatyam](/wiki/Bharatanatyam "Bharatanatyam")*, [Odissi](/wiki/Odissi "Odissi")[[406]](#cite_note-408) and [Manipuri dances](/wiki/Manipuri_dance "Manipuri dance")–native dance traditions have formed across the country.[[402]](#cite_note-arts-404)

[Music of Bangladesh](/wiki/Music_of_Bangladesh "Music of Bangladesh") can be classed into classical, light-classical, [devotional](/wiki/Devotional_music "Devotional music"), and [popular](/wiki/Popular_music "Popular music"). Classical music in Bangladesh is represented by the common forms of devotional music across the Indian subcontinent; such as the [Hindustani classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_classical_music "Hindustani classical music") genre [dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad") and [khayal](/wiki/Khayal "Khayal"). Other major forms include [qawwali](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali") and [kirtan](/wiki/Kirtan "Kirtan").[[402]](#cite_note-arts-404) [Rabindra Sangeet](/wiki/Rabindra_Sangeet "Rabindra Sangeet") and [Nazrul Sangeet](/wiki/Nazrul_geeti "Nazrul geeti") retain their popularity.[[407]](#cite_note-409) Native [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") features the [baul](/wiki/Baul "Baul") mystical tradition, which was popularised by [Lalon](/wiki/Lalon "Lalon") in the 18th century, and is listed by UNESCO as a [Masterpiece of Intangible Cultural Heritage](/wiki/Masterpiece_of_the_Oral_and_Intangible_Heritage_of_Humanity "Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity").[[408]](#cite_note-wei-410)[[409]](#cite_note-alom-411) Other native lyric-based forms of folk music include [bhatiali](/wiki/Bhatiali "Bhatiali"), [bhawaiya](/wiki/Bhawaiya "Bhawaiya"), [dhamail](/wiki/Dhamail "Dhamail"), [kavigan](/wiki/Kavigan "Kavigan"), [jarigan](/wiki/Jarigan "Jarigan"), [sari gan](/wiki/Sari_gan "Sari gan"), marfati, and [gombhira](/wiki/Gombhira "Gombhira").[[402]](#cite_note-arts-404)[[409]](#cite_note-alom-411)

Folk music is accompanied by instruments such as the [ektara](/wiki/Ektara "Ektara"), [dotara](/wiki/Dotara "Dotara"), [dhol](/wiki/Dhol "Dhol"), [bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri") (a type of flute), mandira, [khanjani](/wiki/Khanjani "Khanjani"), [sarinda](/wiki/Sarinda_(instrument) "Sarinda (instrument)"), [khamak](/wiki/Khamak "Khamak"), [dugdugi](/wiki/Duggi_(drum) "Duggi (drum)"), [juri](/wiki/Jori_(instrument) "Jori (instrument)"), jhunjhuni and [majira](/wiki/Taal_(instrument) "Taal (instrument)") (a type of [cymbal](/wiki/Cymbal "Cymbal")).[[409]](#cite_note-alom-411) Bangladesh has a rich tradition of [Indian classical music](/wiki/Indian_classical_music "Indian classical music"), which uses instruments like the [sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar"), tabla, [sarod](/wiki/Sarod "Sarod"), and [santoor](/wiki/Santoor "Santoor").[[410]](#cite_note-412) Musical organisations and schools such as the [Bangladesh Shilpakala Academy](/wiki/Bangladesh_Shilpakala_Academy "Bangladesh Shilpakala Academy") and [Chhayanaut](/wiki/Chhayanaut "Chhayanaut") have played significant roles in preserving the traditions of Bengali folk music.[[409]](#cite_note-alom-411)

[Sabina Yasmin](/wiki/Sabina_Yasmin "Sabina Yasmin") and [Runa Laila](/wiki/Runa_Laila "Runa Laila") are considered two of the greatest female playback singers in the country.[[411]](#cite_note-413) [Andrew Kishore](/wiki/Andrew_Kishore "Andrew Kishore"), another leading playback singer, is considered the "King of Playback".[[412]](#cite_note-414) [Azam Khan](/wiki/Azam_Khan_(singer) "Azam Khan (singer)"), nicknamed the "Pop Samrat" and the "Rock Guru", is a founding figure of [Bangladeshi rock](/wiki/Bangladeshi_rock "Bangladeshi rock").[[413]](#cite_note-415) Musicians such as [Ayub Bachchu](/wiki/Ayub_Bachchu "Ayub Bachchu") and [James](/wiki/James_(musician) "James (musician)") have also gained nationwide popularity.[[414]](#cite_note-416)[[415]](#cite_note-417) [Shayan Chowdhury Arnob](/wiki/Shayan_Chowdhury_Arnob "Shayan Chowdhury Arnob") has been an influential figure in [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock").[[416]](#cite_note-418) Popular pop singers in the 21st century include [Habib Wahid](/wiki/Habib_Wahid "Habib Wahid") and [Tahsan Rahman Khan](/wiki/Tahsan_Rahman_Khan "Tahsan Rahman Khan").[[417]](#cite_note-419)[[418]](#cite_note-420) Influential [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") include [Artcell](/wiki/Artcell "Artcell") and [Warfaze](/wiki/Warfaze "Warfaze").[[419]](#cite_note-421)[[420]](#cite_note-422)

### Media and cinema

Main articles: [Media of Bangladesh](/wiki/Media_of_Bangladesh "Media of Bangladesh") and [Cinema of Bangladesh](/wiki/Cinema_of_Bangladesh "Cinema of Bangladesh")

The history of press in Bangladesh dates back to 1860, when the first printing press was established in Dhaka.[[421]](#cite_note-dr-423) The media in Bangladesh is diverse, competitive, commercial and profitable.[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424) Prominent news agencies in Bangladesh include [Bangladesh Sangbad Sangstha](/wiki/Bangladesh_Sangbad_Sangstha "Bangladesh Sangbad Sangstha") (BSS) and [Bdnews24.com](/wiki/Bdnews24.com "Bdnews24.com").[[423]](#cite_note-bbc-425) Television is the most popular form of media consumption.[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424) [Bangladesh Television](/wiki/Bangladesh_Television "Bangladesh Television") (BTV) is the sole state-owned television network with nationwide coverage.[[424]](#cite_note-426) Private television networks include [ATN Bangla](/wiki/ATN_Bangla "ATN Bangla"), [Channel I](/wiki/Channel_I "Channel I"), [NTV](/wiki/NTV_(Bangladeshi_TV_channel) "NTV (Bangladeshi TV channel)"), [RTV](/wiki/RTV_(Bangladeshi_TV_channel) "RTV (Bangladeshi TV channel)"), [Ekushey TV](/wiki/Ekushey_TV "Ekushey TV"), [Ekattor TV](/wiki/Ekattor_TV "Ekattor TV"), [Jamuna TV](/wiki/Jamuna_TV "Jamuna TV") and [Somoy TV](/wiki/Somoy_TV "Somoy TV").[[423]](#cite_note-bbc-425) Print media is the second-most widely consumed, and newspapers are privately owned and outspoken, including [The Daily Star](/wiki/The_Daily_Star_(Bangladesh) "The Daily Star (Bangladesh)"), [Dhaka Tribune](/wiki/Dhaka_Tribune "Dhaka Tribune"), [The Financial Express](/wiki/The_Financial_Express_(Bangladesh) "The Financial Express (Bangladesh)"), [Bangladesh Pratidin](/wiki/Bangladesh_Pratidin "Bangladesh Pratidin"), [Kaler Kantho](/wiki/Kaler_Kantho "Kaler Kantho"), [Prothom Alo](/wiki/Prothom_Alo "Prothom Alo"), [The Daily Ittefaq](/wiki/The_Daily_Ittefaq "The Daily Ittefaq") and [Jugantor](/wiki/Jugantor "Jugantor").[[423]](#cite_note-bbc-425)[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Anwar_Hossain_in_the_film_%22Nawab_Sirajuddoulah%22_%281967%29.jpg/250px-Anwar_Hossain_in_the_film_%22Nawab_Sirajuddoulah%22_%281967%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anwar_Hossain_in_the_film_%22Nawab_Sirajuddoulah%22_(1967).jpg)

[Anwar Hossain](/wiki/Anwar_Hossain_(actor) "Anwar Hossain (actor)") playing [Siraj-ud-Daulah](/wiki/Siraj-ud-Daulah "Siraj-ud-Daulah"), the last independent [Nawab of Bengal](/wiki/Nawabs_of_Bengal "Nawabs of Bengal"), in the 1967 film *[Nawab Sirajuddaulah](/wiki/Nawab_Sirajuddaula_(film) "Nawab Sirajuddaula (film)")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Headquarters_of_Ekushey_Television%2C_Kawran_Bazar%2C_Dhaka.jpg/250px-Headquarters_of_Ekushey_Television%2C_Kawran_Bazar%2C_Dhaka.jpg)](/wiki/File:Headquarters_of_Ekushey_Television,_Kawran_Bazar,_Dhaka.jpg)

Headquarters of [Ekushey Television](/wiki/Ekushey_Television "Ekushey Television") on a high-rise in [Kawran Bazar](/wiki/Kawran_Bazar "Kawran Bazar"), Dhaka

[Bangladesh Betar](/wiki/Bangladesh_Betar "Bangladesh Betar") is the lone state-run radio service.[[425]](#cite_note-427)[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424) [Radio Foorti](/wiki/Radio_Foorti "Radio Foorti"), [Radio Today](/wiki/Radio_Today "Radio Today"), [Radio Aamar](/wiki/Radio_Aamar "Radio Aamar") and ABC Radio were popular privately owned radio stations; popularity of radio has declined significantly.[[423]](#cite_note-bbc-425)[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424) Popular foreign media include [BBC News](/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News") ([BBC Bangla](/wiki/BBC_Bangla "BBC Bangla")), [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN"), [VOA](/wiki/VOA "VOA") and [Al Jazeera](/wiki/Al_Jazeera "Al Jazeera").[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424) [Indian television drama](/wiki/Indian_television_drama "Indian television drama") in particular has established a "cultural hegemony" over Bangladeshi satellite television.[[426]](#cite_note-428)[[422]](#cite_note-bbc1-424) [Freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press_in_Bangladesh "Freedom of the press in Bangladesh") remains a major concern due to government attempts at censorship and the harassment of journalists. Bangladesh ranked 149th out of the 180 countries listed in the 2025 [World Press Freedom Index](/wiki/World_Press_Freedom_Index "World Press Freedom Index"), among the lowest rankings in the world.[[427]](#cite_note-429)

The [cinema of Bangladesh](/wiki/Cinema_of_Bangladesh "Cinema of Bangladesh") dates back to a screening of a [bioscope](/wiki/Movie_camera "Movie camera") in 1898.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 129[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) The [Nawabs of Dhaka](/wiki/Nawab_of_Dhaka "Nawab of Dhaka") patronised the production of several [silent films](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") from the 1900s.[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) Picture House, the first permanent cinema in Dhaka, began its operation during the year between 1913 and 1914.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130 *[Sukumari](/wiki/Sukumari_(film) "Sukumari (film)")* (The Good Girl), released in 1929, was the first film produced in Bangladesh.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130 *[The Last Kiss](/wiki/The_Last_Kiss_(1931_film) "The Last Kiss (1931 film)")*, the first full-length feature film, was released in 1931.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) By 1947, a total of 80 cinemas were listed.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130 The first Bengali-language film in East Pakistan, *[Mukh O Mukhosh](/wiki/Mukh_O_Mukhosh "Mukh O Mukhosh")* (Face and Mask), was released in 1956.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) *Akash ar Mati* (Sky and Earth), released in 1959, was the second film as such.

The [Bangladesh Film Development Corporation](/wiki/Bangladesh_Film_Development_Corporation "Bangladesh Film Development Corporation") was founded in Dhaka as the East Pakistan Film Development Corporation in 1957–1958, as the full-service film production studio.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) [Zahir Raihan](/wiki/Zahir_Raihan "Zahir Raihan") made various influential films throughout the period, notably *[Kokhono Asheni](/wiki/Kokhono_Asheni "Kokhono Asheni")* in 1961, *Shangam* in 1964 (the first [colour film](/wiki/Color_print_film "Color print film") in Pakistan), and *Jibon Theke Neowa* in 1970.[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) The film industry in Dhaka positioned itself as the base for a Bengali Muslim cinema from the 1960s and onwards.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130 The first movie post-Independence, *[Ora Egaro Jon](/wiki/Ora_Egaro_Jon "Ora Egaro Jon")*, was directed by [Chashi Nazrul Islam](/wiki/Chashi_Nazrul_Islam "Chashi Nazrul Islam") and released in 1972.[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) At the industry's peak, about 80 movies were produced each year between 1996 and 2003, a number which has constantly declined thereafter.[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130[[429]](#cite_note-back-431) Prominent directors include [Khan Ataur Rahman](/wiki/Khan_Ataur_Rahman "Khan Ataur Rahman"), [Alamgir Kabir](/wiki/Alamgir_Kabir_(film_maker) "Alamgir Kabir (film maker)"), [Amjad Hossain](/wiki/Amjad_Hossain "Amjad Hossain"), [Humayun Ahmed](/wiki/Humayun_Ahmed "Humayun Ahmed"), [Morshedul Islam](/wiki/Morshedul_Islam "Morshedul Islam"), [Tanvir Mokammel](/wiki/Tanvir_Mokammel "Tanvir Mokammel"), [Tareque Masud](/wiki/Tareque_Masud "Tareque Masud"), [Salahuddin Lavlu](/wiki/Salahuddin_Lavlu "Salahuddin Lavlu") and [Enamul Karim Nirjhar](/wiki/Enamul_Karim_Nirjhar "Enamul Karim Nirjhar").[[428]](#cite_note-easmin-430): 130 Tareque Masud was honoured by [FIPRESCI](/wiki/FIPRESCI "FIPRESCI") at the 2002 [Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/Cannes_Film_Festival "Cannes Film Festival") for his film *[Matir Moina](/wiki/Matir_Moina "Matir Moina")* (The Clay Bird). [Film societes](/wiki/Film_society "Film society") have played a crucial role in the development of cinema in Bangladesh.[[430]](#cite_note-432)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Bangladeshi cuisine](/wiki/Bangladeshi_cuisine "Bangladeshi cuisine")

Further information: [Bengali cuisine](/wiki/Bengali_cuisine "Bengali cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Kala_bhuna_1.jpg/250px-Kala_bhuna_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kala_bhuna_1.jpg)

*[Kala bhuna](/wiki/Kala_bhuna "Kala bhuna")*, a popular [beef dish](/wiki/List_of_beef_dishes "List of beef dishes") from [Chittagong Division](/wiki/Chittagong_Division "Chittagong Division")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%87_%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9B%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0_%E0%A6%AD%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE.jpg/250px-%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%87_%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9B%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0_%E0%A6%AD%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E0%A6%B0%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%87_%E0%A6%AE%E0%A6%BE%E0%A6%9B%E0%A7%87%E0%A6%B0_%E0%A6%AD%E0%A7%81%E0%A6%A8%E0%A6%BE.jpg)

[Rohu](/wiki/Rohu "Rohu") curry, a popular fish-based dish

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Morog_Polao_1.jpg/250px-Morog_Polao_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Morog_Polao_1.jpg)

Chicken [pilaf](/wiki/Pilaf "Pilaf") (*Morog Polao*), a traditional dish from [Old Dhaka](/wiki/Old_Dhaka "Old Dhaka")

Bangladeshi cuisine, formed by the nation's geographic location, climate, and history—is rich and diverse.[[431]](#cite_note-cuisine-433): 14 The staple dish is [white rice](/wiki/White_rice "White rice"), which along with fish, forms the culinary base. Varieties of [leaf vegetables](/wiki/Leaf_vegetable "Leaf vegetable"), potatoes, [gourds](/wiki/Gourd "Gourd") and [lentils](/wiki/Lentil "Lentil") ([dal](/wiki/Dal "Dal")) also play an important role. [Curries](/wiki/Curry "Curry") of beef, [mutton](/wiki/Mutton "Mutton"), chicken and duck are commonly consumed,[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434) along with multiple types of [bhortas](/wiki/Bhurta "Bhurta") (mashed vegetables),[[433]](#cite_note-435) *bhajis* ([stir fried](/wiki/Stir_frying "Stir frying") vegetables) and [tarkaris](/wiki/Tarkari "Tarkari") ([curried](/wiki/Curry "Curry") vegetables).[[431]](#cite_note-cuisine-433): 8 Mughal-influenced dishes include [kormas](/wiki/Korma "Korma"), kalias, [biryanis](/wiki/Biryani "Biryani"), [pulaos](/wiki/Pilaf "Pilaf"), [teharis](/wiki/Tahri_(dish) "Tahri (dish)") and [khichuris](/wiki/Khichuri "Khichuri").[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434)

Among the various used spices, [turmeric](/wiki/Turmeric "Turmeric"), [fenugreek](/wiki/Fenugreek "Fenugreek"), [nigella](/wiki/Nigella "Nigella"), [coriander](/wiki/Coriander "Coriander"), [anise](/wiki/Anise "Anise"), [cardamom](/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom") and [chili powder](/wiki/Chili_powder "Chili powder") are widely used; a famous spice mix is the [panch phoron](/wiki/Panch_phoron "Panch phoron"). Condiments and herbs used include [red onions](/wiki/Red_onion "Red onion"), [green chillies](/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper"), garlic, [ginger](/wiki/Ginger "Ginger"), [cilantro](/wiki/Cilantro "Cilantro"), and [mint](/wiki/Mentha "Mentha").[[431]](#cite_note-cuisine-433): 12 [Coconut milk](/wiki/Coconut_milk "Coconut milk"), [mustard paste](/wiki/Mustard_(condiment) "Mustard (condiment)"), [mustard seeds](/wiki/Mustard_seeds "Mustard seeds"), [mustard oil](/wiki/Mustard_oil "Mustard oil"), [ghee](/wiki/Ghee "Ghee"), [achars](/wiki/South_Asian_pickle "South Asian pickle")[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434) and [chutneys](/wiki/Chutney "Chutney") are also widely used in the cuisine.[[431]](#cite_note-cuisine-433): 13–14

Fish is the main source of protein, owing to the country's riverine geography, and it is often enjoyed with its [roe](/wiki/Roe "Roe"). The [hilsa](/wiki/Hilsa "Hilsa") is the national fish and is immensely popular. Other highly consumed fishes include [rohu](/wiki/Rohu "Rohu"), [pangas](/wiki/Pangasius_pangasius "Pangasius pangasius"), and [tilapia](/wiki/Tilapia "Tilapia").[[434]](#cite_note-436) [Lobsters](/wiki/Lobster "Lobster"), [shrimps](/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp") and [dried fish](/wiki/Dried_fish "Dried fish") (*shutki*) also play an important role in the local cuisines.[[431]](#cite_note-cuisine-433): 8 In Chittagong, famous dishes involving beef include [kala bhuna](/wiki/Kala_bhuna "Kala bhuna") and [mezban](/wiki/Mezban "Mezban"), the latter being a traditionally popular feast, featuring the serving of *mezbani gosht*, a hot and spicy beef curry.[[431]](#cite_note-cuisine-433): 10[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434)[[435]](#cite_note-guardiancuisine-437) In Sylhet, the *[shatkora](/wiki/Shatkora "Shatkora")* lemons are used to marinate dishes, a notable one is [beef hatkora](/wiki/Beef_Hatkhora "Beef Hatkhora").[[435]](#cite_note-guardiancuisine-437) Among the tribal communities in the [Chittagong Hill Tracts](/wiki/Chittagong_Hill_Tracts "Chittagong Hill Tracts"), cooking with [bamboo shoots](/wiki/Bamboo_shoot "Bamboo shoot") is popular.[[436]](#cite_note-438) Khulna is renowned for using *chui jhal* ([piper chaba](/wiki/Piper_chaba "Piper chaba")) in its meat-based dishes.[[435]](#cite_note-guardiancuisine-437)[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434) *Kalai ruti*, a type of rustic [flatbread](/wiki/Flatbread "Flatbread"), is widely consumed in [Chapai Nawabganj](/wiki/Chapai_Nawabganj "Chapai Nawabganj") of Rajshahi.[[437]](#cite_note-439)

Bangladesh has a vast spread of desserts, including distinctive [sweets](/wiki/Sweets "Sweets") such as the *[rôshogolla](/wiki/Rasgulla "Rasgulla")*, *[roshmalai](/wiki/Ras_malai "Ras malai")*, *[chomchom](/wiki/Chomchom "Chomchom")*, *[sondesh](/wiki/Sandesh_(confectionery) "Sandesh (confectionery)")*, *[mishti doi](/wiki/Mishti_doi "Mishti doi")* and *[kalojaam](/wiki/Gulab_jamun "Gulab jamun")*, and *[jilapi](/wiki/Jalebi "Jalebi")*.[[438]](#cite_note-440) [Pithas](/wiki/Pitha "Pitha") are traditional boiled desserts made with rice or fruits.[[439]](#cite_note-441) [Halwa](/wiki/Halwa "Halwa"), [shemai](/wiki/Shemai "Shemai") and [falooda](/wiki/Falooda "Falooda"), the latter two being a variation of [vermicelli](/wiki/Vermicelli "Vermicelli"); are popular desserts during religious festivities.[[440]](#cite_note-442)[[441]](#cite_note-443)[[442]](#cite_note-444) [Ruti](/wiki/Roti "Roti"), [naan](/wiki/Naan "Naan"), [paratha](/wiki/Paratha "Paratha"), [luchi](/wiki/Luchi "Luchi") and [bakarkhani](/wiki/Bakarkhani "Bakarkhani") are the main local breads.[[443]](#cite_note-445)[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434) Hot [milk tea](/wiki/Milk_tea "Milk tea") is the most commonly consumed beverage in the country, being at the centre of [group conversations](/wiki/Adda_(South_Asian) "Adda (South Asian)").[[444]](#cite_note-446) [Borhani](/wiki/Borhani "Borhani"), [mattha](/wiki/Mattha "Mattha") and [lassi](/wiki/Lassi "Lassi") are popular traditionally consumed beverages.[[445]](#cite_note-447)[[446]](#cite_note-448) [Kebabs](/wiki/Kebab "Kebab") are widely popular, particularly [seekh kebab](/wiki/Seekh_kebab "Seekh kebab"), [chapli kebab](/wiki/Chapli_kebab "Chapli kebab"), [shami kebab](/wiki/Shami_kebab "Shami kebab"), [chicken tikka](/wiki/Chicken_tikka "Chicken tikka") and [shashlik](/wiki/Shashlik "Shashlik"), along with various types of *chaaps*.[[432]](#cite_note-shaheda-434) Popular street foods include [chotpoti](/wiki/Chotpoti "Chotpoti"), [jhal muri](/wiki/Jhal_muri "Jhal muri"), *shingara*,[[447]](#cite_note-449) [samosa](/wiki/Samosa "Samosa") and [fuchka](/wiki/Panipuri "Panipuri").[[448]](#cite_note-450)

### Sports

Main article: [Sports in Bangladesh](/wiki/Sports_in_Bangladesh "Sports in Bangladesh")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Mashrafe_2016_%289%29.jpg/250px-Mashrafe_2016_%289%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mashrafe_2016_(9).jpg)

[Bangladesh cricket team](/wiki/Bangladesh_cricket_team "Bangladesh cricket team")

In rural Bangladesh, several [traditional indigenous sports](/wiki/Bengali_traditional_games "Bengali traditional games") such as [Kabaddi](/wiki/Kabaddi "Kabaddi"), [Boli Khela](/wiki/Boli_Khela "Boli Khela"), [Lathi Khela](/wiki/Lathi_Khela "Lathi Khela") and [Nouka Baich](/wiki/Nouka_Baich "Nouka Baich") remain fairly popular. While Kabaddi is the national sport,[[449]](#cite_note-451) [Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") is arguably the most popular sport in the country. The [national cricket team](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_cricket_team "Bangladesh national cricket team") participated in their first [Cricket World Cup](/wiki/Cricket_World_Cup "Cricket World Cup") in 1999 and the following year was granted [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") status. Bangladesh reached the quarter-final of the [2015 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2015_Cricket_World_Cup "2015 Cricket World Cup"), the semi-final of the [2017 ICC Champions Trophy](/wiki/2017_ICC_Champions_Trophy "2017 ICC Champions Trophy") and they reached the final of the [Asia Cup](/wiki/Asia_Cup "Asia Cup") 3 times – in 2012, 2016, and 2018. [Shakib Al Hasan](/wiki/Shakib_Al_Hasan "Shakib Al Hasan") is widely regarded as one of the greatest [all-rounders](/wiki/All-rounder "All-rounder") in the history of the sport.[[450]](#cite_note-452) In 2020, the [Bangladesh national under-19 cricket team](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_under-19_cricket_team "Bangladesh national under-19 cricket team") won the men's [Under-19 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2020_Under-19_Cricket_World_Cup "2020 Under-19 Cricket World Cup").[[451]](#cite_note-453)[[452]](#cite_note-454) The [Bangladesh national under-19 cricket team](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_under-19_cricket_team "Bangladesh national under-19 cricket team") also won the [U-19 Asia cup](/wiki/Under-19_Men%27s_Asia_Cup "Under-19 Men's Asia Cup") in 2023 and 2024 consecutively.[[453]](#cite_note-455)[[454]](#cite_note-456) In 2018, the [Bangladesh women's national cricket team](/wiki/Bangladesh_women%27s_national_cricket_team "Bangladesh women's national cricket team") won the [2018 Women's Twenty20 Asia Cup](/wiki/2018_Women%27s_Twenty20_Asia_Cup "2018 Women's Twenty20 Asia Cup").[[455]](#cite_note-457)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Bangladesh_National_Football_Team_in_Maldives_in_the_SAFF_Championship_2021.jpg/250px-Bangladesh_National_Football_Team_in_Maldives_in_the_SAFF_Championship_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bangladesh_National_Football_Team_in_Maldives_in_the_SAFF_Championship_2021.jpg)

[Bangladesh football team](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_football_team "Bangladesh national football team")

[Football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") is the second-most popular sport in Bangladesh, following cricket.[[456]](#cite_note-458) The first instance of a national football team was the emergence of the [Shadhin Bangla football team](/wiki/Shadhin_Bangla_football_team "Shadhin Bangla football team") during the liberation war of 1971.[[457]](#cite_note-459) On 25 July 1971, the team's captain, [Zakaria Pintoo](/wiki/Zakaria_Pintoo "Zakaria Pintoo"), became the first person to hoist the Bangladesh flag on foreign land before their match in neighbouring India.[[458]](#cite_note-460) Following independence, the [national football team](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_football_team "Bangladesh national football team") made its debut in 1973 and eventually achieved the feat of participating in the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") (1980), becoming only the second South Asian team to do so.[[459]](#cite_note-461) Bangladesh's most notable achievements in football include the [2003 SAFF Gold Cup](/wiki/2003_SAFF_Gold_Cup "2003 SAFF Gold Cup") and [1999 South Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1999_South_Asian_Games "Football at the 1999 South Asian Games"). The [Bangladesh women's national football team](/wiki/Bangladesh_women%27s_national_football_team "Bangladesh women's national football team") won the [SAFF Women's Championship](/wiki/SAFF_Women%27s_Championship "SAFF Women's Championship") consecutively in 2022 and 2024.[[460]](#cite_note-462)

Bangladesh archers Ety Khatun and Roman Sana won several gold medals winning all the 10 [archery](/wiki/Archery "Archery") events (both individual and team events) in the [2019 South Asian Games](/wiki/2019_South_Asian_Games "2019 South Asian Games").[[461]](#cite_note-463) The [National Sports Council](/wiki/National_Sports_Council "National Sports Council") regulates 42 sporting federations.[[462]](#cite_note-464) [Chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") is very popular in Bangladesh. Bangladesh has five grandmasters in chess. Among them, [Niaz Murshed](/wiki/Niaz_Murshed "Niaz Murshed") was the first grandmaster in South Asia.[[463]](#cite_note-465) In 2010, mountain climber [Musa Ibrahim](/wiki/Musa_Ibrahim "Musa Ibrahim") became the first Bangladeshi climber to conquer [Mount Everest](/wiki/Mount_Everest "Mount Everest").[[464]](#cite_note-dailystardetail-466) [Wasfia Nazreen](/wiki/Wasfia_Nazreen "Wasfia Nazreen") is the first Bangladeshi climber to climb the [Seven Summits](/wiki/Seven_Summits "Seven Summits") and the [K2](/wiki/K2 "K2").[[465]](#cite_note-467)[[466]](#cite_note-468)

32. **[^](#cite_ref-34)** Dyson, Tim (2018). *A population history of India: from the first modern people to the present day* (1st ed.). Oxford: Oxford University Press. pp. 80–81. "However, in assessing the likely scale of the mortality ... there were no demographic data for any significant part of Bengal. Indeed, many Company administrators seldom ventured from Calcutta. The few local reports on famine deaths were for small and unrepresentative populations. Also, as we noted in Chapter 4, cultivators could be very mobile, and they often abandoned their villages at times of famine. Much of any 'depopulation' was undoubtedly caused by out-migration. In addition, birth rates tend to fall during famines, and this too would have reduced the population ... To sum up, the 1769–70 famine was certainly exceptional in terms of Bengal's experience during the eighteenth century (although crop losses from a cyclone and a shift in a river course also contributed to many famine deaths in 1787–88). It is very unlikely, however, that the 1769–70 crisis involved the deaths of 10 million people. Indeed, on Datta's assessment, even a figure of 5 million may well lie outside the plausible range. However, famine mortality and large-scale out-migration did cause significant depopulation in large parts of Bengal—from which it evidently took several years to recover. As late as 1773, Company officials regarded the revival of the province's economy as requiring substantial return-migration from adjacent territories—including the, then, independent state of Awadh."
